background_cases: A surveyor measures the height of a cathedral spire with calibrated instruments, believes it to be 65 metres tall, and is correct.
A historian consults multiple independent archives and forms the belief that the treaty was signed in 1713, which is true.
A chemist performs a litmus test, observes the paper turn red, believes the solution is acidic, and the solution is indeed acidic.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S is justified in believing that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
counterexample: A man glances at a clock on a public building and forms the belief that it is exactly two o'clock. The clock is in fact stopped and has been stopped for some days, but by sheer coincidence the man happens to look at it at precisely two o'clock. His belief is true, he holds it sincerely, and his justification — reading a normally reliable public timepiece — is of the ordinary sort we accept in everyday life.
abductive_insight: The background cases each exhibited a non-accidental connection between the fact believed and the ground of belief, yet the definition captured only the presence of justification, not its actually producing the truth of the belief in the right way. What the abstraction lost was the requirement that it not be a matter of luck that the justified belief turned out true.
passage:
We may begin by considering several cases in which it seems uncontroversial that a person possesses knowledge. A surveyor, employing calibrated instruments and established methods, measures the height of a cathedral spire and arrives at the figure of sixty-five metres; the spire is indeed sixty-five metres tall, and we should say without hesitation that the surveyor knows this. Again, a historian who consults multiple independent archives and thereby forms the belief that a certain treaty was signed in 1713 — the date being correct — is rightly said to know when the treaty was signed. Or consider the chemist who performs a litmus test, observes the paper turn red, and concludes that the solution is acidic, which it is. In each of these instances the subject holds a true belief and possesses what we should ordinarily regard as adequate justification for holding it.

From such paradigm cases one might extract the following analysis: a subject S knows that p if and only if p is true, S believes that p, and S is justified in believing that p. This tripartite account has considerable intuitive appeal, and indeed it captures everything that is explicitly present in the cases just surveyed. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man glances at a clock mounted on the facade of a public building and reads the time as two o'clock; he thereupon believes that it is two o'clock, and he is right — it is exactly two o'clock. However, the clock has been stopped for several days and shows two o'clock at all hours. It is purely by accident that the man happened to consult it at the one moment in the day when it displayed the correct time. His belief is true, and his justification — namely, reading an apparently functioning public timepiece — is of precisely the sort we accept in ordinary contexts. Nevertheless, we should be most reluctant to say that he knows what time it is. The background cases all exhibited a feature which the definition failed to record: in each of them, the connection between the justification and the truth of the belief was not merely coincidental. The surveyor's instruments were causally responsive to the actual height of the spire; the historian's archives bore a reliable evidential relation to the historical facts. The tripartite analysis, by requiring only that justified belief and truth obtain simultaneously, neglects to demand that the justification be appropriately connected to the fact believed, and it is this omission that permits cases of lucky coincidence to satisfy the conditions whilst plainly failing to constitute knowledge.

background_cases: A birdwatcher with decades of experience identifies a bird in good light as a goldfinch, and it is indeed a goldfinch.
A radiologist reads a chest X-ray using well-practised diagnostic techniques and correctly identifies a fracture.
A native speaker of French hears a sentence and immediately and correctly judges it to be grammatically well-formed.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p was formed by a cognitive process that is reliable — that is, one which produces a high ratio of true beliefs to false beliefs.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process
counterexample: Consider a person raised on a planet where the atmosphere refracts light in such a way that objects routinely appear to be in locations other than their true ones. On that planet, his perceptual faculties produce overwhelmingly false beliefs about the positions of middle-sized objects — they are unreliable in that environment. He is then transported, unknowingly, to Earth, where conditions are normal and his perceptual faculties happen to function with high accuracy. He looks at a barn and believes it is directly in front of him, which it is.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved processes operating in the environment in which their reliability had been established. The definition abstracts away the dependency on environment, recording only that the process be reliable simpliciter, and thereby fails to specify whether reliability must hold in the environment where the belief is actually formed or in the environment in which the process was developed.
passage:
Let us take note of several unproblematic instances of knowledge. A birdwatcher, possessed of decades of experience and practised skill, identifies a bird in good light as a goldfinch, and a goldfinch it is; we say he knows it to be a goldfinch. A radiologist, trained in the interpretation of chest X-rays, reads a film and correctly identifies a fracture; she knows there is a fracture. A native speaker of French hears a sentence and immediately judges it to be grammatically well-formed, and the judgement is correct; one would not hesitate to ascribe knowledge to her. In each case the subject arrives at a true belief through a cognitive process — perceptual recognition, trained diagnostic inference, linguistic competence — that characteristically produces true beliefs in the relevant domain.

One might accordingly propose that knowledge is true belief produced by a reliable cognitive process, where reliability is understood as the tendency of the process to yield a high ratio of true beliefs to false beliefs. This reliabilist account seems well suited to the cases above, for in each instance the process in question has an impressive track record of accuracy. However, there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a person has been raised on a distant planet where atmospheric refraction systematically distorts the apparent positions of objects, so that his visual faculties produce overwhelmingly false beliefs about spatial location in that environment — they are, by any reasonable standard, unreliable there. He is then transported, without his knowledge, to Earth, where conditions of observation are normal and his perceptual faculties happen to function with a high degree of accuracy. He looks at a barn and forms the belief that a barn stands directly before him, which is true, and his visual system is, in the terrestrial environment, a reliable producer of such beliefs. Yet there is something deeply unsatisfying about crediting him with knowledge. The difficulty is that the definition requires only that the process be reliable, without specifying the environment in which its reliability is to be assessed. In all of the background cases the process had been exercised and had demonstrated its reliability in the very environment in which the belief was formed. The abstraction from cases to definition lost this environmental anchoring, and the result is an analysis that can be satisfied by a process that is reliable only by the accident of the subject's having been placed in a congenial environment rather than by any stable epistemic virtue.

background_cases: A juror believes the defendant is guilty on the basis of compelling eyewitness testimony, forensic evidence, and the defendant's own partial admission; there are no countervailing considerations, and the defendant is indeed guilty.

A scientist believes that a chemical compound has a certain molecular structure on the basis of multiple convergent spectroscopic analyses, with no anomalous data, and the structure is as she believes.

A navigator believes the ship is at a certain latitude on the basis of a careful celestial observation, confirmed by a second independent sighting, with no reason to doubt either observation, and the ship is indeed at that latitude.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) there is no genuine defeater — that is, no true proposition which, were S to become aware of it, would undermine S's justification for believing that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
There exists no true proposition that would, if known by S, undermine S's justification for p
counterexample: A doctor examines a patient and correctly diagnoses a rare condition on the basis of textbook symptoms. There exists a true report, published in an obscure foreign journal, which describes a superficially similar but distinct condition presenting with the very same symptoms in a small number of cases. Were the doctor to encounter this report, it would reasonably undermine her confidence in the original diagnosis. Yet the report, though genuine, concerns a condition that is not present in this patient; her original diagnosis is correct and well-founded.
abductive_insight: The background cases shared the feature that any true proposition capable of undermining the subject's justification would also have pointed towards the belief's being false. The definition failed to distinguish between defeaters that genuinely indicate error and those that merely appear to — it demands the absence of all true undermining propositions, even those that are misleading.
passage:
It is instructive to consider cases in which knowledge appears most secure. A juror believes the defendant guilty upon the strength of compelling eyewitness testimony, corroborating forensic evidence, and the defendant's own partial admission; there are no countervailing considerations, and the defendant is in fact guilty. A scientist believes that a chemical compound possesses a certain molecular structure, her belief resting upon multiple convergent spectroscopic analyses with no anomalous results, and the structure is indeed as she supposes. A navigator, having taken a careful celestial observation confirmed by an independent sighting, believes the ship to be at a certain latitude, and so it is. In each of these cases the subject holds a justified true belief, and there appears to be no true proposition lurking in the background which, were the subject to learn of it, would rationally require the abandonment of the belief.

One might therefore propose that knowledge is justified true belief for which there exists no genuine defeater — no true proposition which, were the subject to become aware of it, would undermine the justification for the belief in question. This no-defeater condition appears to capture what distinguishes the foregoing cases from mere lucky true belief. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a doctor examines a patient and correctly diagnoses a rare condition on the basis of textbook symptoms. It happens that a true report, published in an obscure foreign journal, describes a superficially similar but distinct condition that presents with precisely the same symptoms in a small number of patients. Were the doctor to encounter this report, it would quite reasonably shake her confidence in the original diagnosis and might lead her to suspend judgement pending further tests. The no-defeater condition is thus violated: there exists a true proposition — the content of the report — which would undermine her justification. And yet her diagnosis is correct; the similar condition described in the report is not present in this patient; her evidence genuinely supports the conclusion she has drawn. We should, it seems, wish to say that she knows what ails her patient. The background cases all enjoyed the feature that any true proposition capable of undermining the subject's justification would also have indicated that the belief was in fact false. The definition, in demanding the absence of all true undermining propositions, failed to discriminate between defeaters that reveal a genuine deficiency in the subject's epistemic position and those that are merely misleading — true propositions that would confuse but would not track any actual error.

background_cases: A child touches a hot stove, feels pain, and thereby comes to believe that the stove is hot; the stove's being hot causes the sensation which causes the belief.
A person sees a red post-box in good light and forms the belief that there is a red post-box before him; the presence of the post-box causes, via normal visual processes, his belief.

A geologist examines a rock formation and, through a chain of perception and inference causally initiated by the formation's features, comes to believe that the strata were deposited in the Jurassic period, which they were.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is caused by the fact that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is caused by the fact that p
counterexample: A man believes that a certain building is on fire. The building is indeed on fire, and moreover the fire is the cause of his belief — but only in the following deviant way. The fire causes a short circuit in the building's electrical system, which in turn causes the fire alarm to malfunction and remain silent. The silence of the alarm causes a passing electrician to investigate the wiring, and the electrician, upon discovering the short circuit, mentions it casually to the man in the street, who infers — somewhat irrationally and by a leap of imagination — that the building must be on fire.
abductive_insight: In the background cases, the causal chain from fact to belief ran through processes — perception, trained inference — that were such as to make the belief's content sensitive to the fact's obtaining. The definition required only that the fact cause the belief, without stipulating that the causal chain be of an epistemically appropriate kind.
passage:
There are cases in which the causal ancestry of a belief seems central to its status as knowledge. A child who touches a hot stove and feels pain thereby acquires the belief that the stove is hot; the heat of the stove is the cause, through a natural and intelligible process, of the very belief whose content it constitutes. A person who sees a red post-box in broad daylight and believes there to be a red post-box before him arrives at his belief through a causal process initiated by the object of belief itself. A geologist who examines a rock formation and concludes that the strata were deposited during the Jurassic period traces a causal path from the geological facts through perception and trained inference to her belief. In each instance we are inclined to say that the subject knows, and in each instance there is a causal chain running from the fact believed to the belief about it.

One might therefore propose that knowledge is true belief caused by the fact that makes the belief true. This causal theory has the virtue of explaining why knowledge seems to require more than the mere conjunction of truth and belief — it requires a real connection between the two. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man comes to believe that a certain building is on fire. The building is on fire, and the fire is indeed the distal cause of his belief — but the causal route is deviant. The fire causes a short circuit in the building's wiring; the short circuit causes the fire alarm to malfunction and remain silent; the silence of the alarm causes a passing electrician to investigate the wiring fault; the electrician mentions the fault to the man, who by an irrational leap of imagination infers that the building must be ablaze. The causal condition of the definition is satisfied, for the fact that the building is on fire is a cause — indeed, the initiating cause — of the man's belief. Yet we should hardly wish to say that he knows the building to be on fire; his belief, though caused by the relevant fact, arrives at truth by a route that is epistemically fortuitous. The difficulty is that in all the background cases the causal chain from fact to belief ran through processes — normal perception, disciplined inference — that were constitutively apt for producing beliefs whose content reflects the facts. The definition recorded only that the fact cause the belief, without attending to whether the manner of causation was of the sort that renders belief epistemically responsive to reality.

background_cases: A mathematics student carefully proves a theorem step by step; there is no nearby possibility in which she makes an error, for her understanding of the material is thorough and her attention unwavering.

A man identifies his own bicycle in a rack of twenty bicycles; he has owned it for years and knows its every scratch and marking, so there is no nearby situation in which he would mistake another bicycle for his.

A woman tastes her morning tea and believes it contains milk; her palate is discriminating and the taste of milk is unmistakable to her, so in no close possible situation would she form this belief falsely.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) in nearby possible worlds in which p is false, S does not believe that p — that is, S could not easily have been wrong.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
In nearby possible worlds where p is false, S does not believe that p
counterexample: A man stands before a genuine barn in a region where, unbeknown to him, the countryside is dotted with highly convincing barn facades — mere fronts of barns with nothing behind them. He happens to be looking at the one real barn in the area. In the nearest possible worlds in which there is no real barn before him — worlds in which he is standing before a facade — he would still believe there to be a barn before him, for the facades are visually indistinguishable from genuine barns.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involved a method of belief-formation that was discriminating across the range of relevant local alternatives. The definition required only that the subject would not believe p in nearby worlds where p is false, but it failed to capture that the method must be able to distinguish the actual case from the relevant alternatives present in the subject's environment.
passage:
Let us consider several cases in which a person's true belief appears to be, in some important sense, non-accidental. A mathematics student proves a theorem with care and rigour; her understanding of the material is thorough and her concentration exemplary, so that there is no nearby possibility in which she would have made an error in the proof. We say she knows the theorem. A man identifies his own bicycle in a rack containing twenty others; he has owned it for years and is familiar with every scratch and dent, so that there is no close possible situation in which he would mistake a different bicycle for his own. He knows which bicycle is his. A woman tastes her morning tea and believes it to contain milk; her palate is sufficiently refined that in no proximate situation would she form this belief falsely. She knows the tea contains milk.

From these cases one might extract the condition that knowledge requires safety: in nearby possible worlds in which the proposition believed is false, the subject does not believe it. The knower, on this account, is one who could not easily have been wrong. This condition neatly captures what the foregoing cases share — a robustness of true belief across close possibilities. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man stands before a barn — a perfectly real, ordinary barn — and believes that there is a barn before him. However, the surrounding countryside is, unbeknown to him, populated with elaborate barn facades: mere painted fronts, visually indistinguishable from genuine barns, erected for purposes of a film production. He happens to be looking at the sole real barn in the vicinity. In the nearest possible worlds in which there is no real barn before him — that is, worlds in which he stands before one of the facades — he would still believe himself to be looking at a barn. The safety condition is violated, for he could very easily have been wrong. And yet there is a strong intuitive pull towards saying that, as things actually stand, he is looking at a real barn and his perceptual faculties are functioning normally — surely he sees that it is a barn. The matter is, at the very least, far from clear. What the background cases shared, and what the definition failed to make explicit, was that the method of belief-formation reliably discriminated the target state of affairs from the relevant alternatives present in the actual environment. The safety condition, by attending only to what the subject would believe in counterfactual scenarios, neglected the question of whether the subject's method is appropriately sensitive to the local epistemic landscape.

background_cases: An experienced archer aims carefully at a target and hits the bullseye; her accuracy is attributable to her skill.
A seasoned judge of character, upon meeting a stranger, correctly discerns that the stranger is untrustworthy; her correct assessment is the product of long experience and perceptiveness.
A chess grandmaster foresees, twenty moves in advance, that a particular endgame position will arise, and it does; his foresight is the exercise of deep strategic understanding.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) p is true (the belief is accurate), (iii) S's belief is formed through the exercise of intellectual skill or competence, and (iv) the accuracy of S's belief is attributable to that competence — the belief is accurate because it is adroit.
conditions: S believes that p
p is true
S's belief is formed through the exercise of intellectual competence
The accuracy of the belief is attributable to that competence
counterexample: An expert birdwatcher, exercising her considerable skill, identifies a bird in a distant tree as a species of warbler. She is correct — it is indeed that species of warbler. However, the bird she is actually seeing is a different, visually similar species; the genuine warbler of the kind she identified is concealed behind it in the foliage and is the bird that happens to make her identification true. Her belief is accurate and her skill is genuine, but the accuracy of her identification is not attributable to her competence — it is a coincidence that the bird her skill failed to identify correctly is concealed behind one that makes her belief true.
abductive_insight: The background cases exhibited a tight explanatory connection between the exercise of skill and the obtaining of the outcome: the archer's skill explains the arrow's hitting the bullseye. The definition captured the requirement that accuracy be because of skill, but the concept of 'because of' proves more demanding than mere co-presence of skill and accuracy — it requires that skill be the operant explanation, not a bystander to a lucky outcome.
passage:
We may observe that in certain exemplary cases of knowledge, true belief appears to be the manifestation of a cognitive excellence. An experienced archer aims with care and strikes the bullseye; we credit her success to her skill, not to fortune. Analogously, a seasoned judge of character correctly discerns that a stranger is not to be trusted, and we attribute her insight to long experience and perceptiveness rather than to guesswork. A chess grandmaster foresees, twenty moves in advance, the emergence of a particular endgame position, and when it materialises we recognise his foresight as the fruit of deep strategic understanding. In each case the successful outcome — the accurate belief — is explained by the competence of the agent, and it is this explanatory connection that seems to elevate mere true belief to the status of knowledge.

One might therefore propose that knowledge is apt belief: belief that is accurate because it is adroit, where the truth of the belief is attributable to the intellectual competence manifested in its formation. This virtue-theoretic analysis captures a great deal of what is attractive in the foregoing cases. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose an expert birdwatcher, exercising her considerable ornithological skill, peers through binoculars at a bird in a distant tree and identifies it as a reed warbler. She is right — there is a reed warbler in the tree. But the bird at which she is looking is not, in fact, a reed warbler; it is a visually similar species that her skill, at this distance, has failed to distinguish. The actual reed warbler is concealed in the foliage directly behind the bird she observes, invisible to her. Her belief is accurate, and she has exercised genuine intellectual competence, yet the accuracy of her belief is not attributable to that competence in the way the analysis requires. It is sheer luck that a reed warbler happened to be hidden behind the bird she misidentified. The background cases all exhibited a structure in which the competence of the subject was the operant explanation of the successful outcome — the arrow hit the bullseye because the archer was skilled, not because a gust of wind happened to compensate for a poor shot. The definition attempts to capture this with the locution 'accurate because adroit,' but this explanatory connective proves more exacting than the mere co-presence of skill and accuracy. What the abstraction from cases to definition left underspecified was the precise manner in which competence must explain success, and it is this lacuna that permits cases of skilful but fortuitously accurate belief to slip through.

background_cases: A detective, having gathered extensive physical evidence, eyewitness testimony, and a confession, justifiably believes that the suspect committed the crime; there is no true evidence, known or unknown, that would undermine his case, and the suspect is guilty.

A cartographer, using satellite imagery and ground-truth measurements, believes that a certain river changes course at a particular point; no countervailing data exist, and the river does change course there.

A physician diagnoses a patient with a specific genetic condition after reviewing genetic test results, family history, and clinical presentation; no conflicting evidence exists, and the diagnosis is correct.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) there is no true proposition d such that, if d were added to S's evidence, S would no longer be justified in believing that p, unless d is itself defeated by some further true proposition.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
No undefeated true defeater of S's justification for p exists
counterexample: A woman justifiably believes, on the basis of strong evidence, that her colleague was in Leeds on Tuesday. She once read, in a reliable newspaper, a report confirming her colleague's presence in Leeds that day, but she has entirely forgotten having read it. The report is true and, were she to recall it, would further support her belief. However, suppose there also exists a true proposition — that the newspaper retracted the report the following day owing to an editorial error in attribution, though the retraction was itself mistaken and the original report was accurate. Were the retraction (a true proposition about what the newspaper published) added to her total evidence alongside the half-forgotten report, it would undermine a piece of her justificatory base, even though the original report was correct.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved subjects whose evidence was, so to speak, epistemically tidy — all evidence pointed one way and no forgotten or submerged evidential layers complicated the picture. The definition's recursive defeater-of-defeater structure assumes that all relevant evidence is available for rational adjudication, but it cannot properly handle cases where evidence that the subject once possessed but has forgotten continues to play a quiet supportive role.
passage:
There are cases of knowledge in which the subject's epistemic position seems altogether unassailable. A detective who has amassed physical evidence, eyewitness testimony, and a confession, and against whose case no countervailing evidence exists, knows that the suspect is guilty. A cartographer whose satellite imagery and ground-truth measurements converge upon a single conclusion, with no anomalous data in sight, knows where the river changes course. A physician whose genetic tests, family history, and clinical presentation all point unambiguously to a diagnosis, with no conflicting results, knows what ails the patient. In each case there appears to be no true proposition, known or unknown, that would rationally undermine the subject's justified belief.

One might accordingly refine the traditional analysis by adding a no-undefeated-defeater condition: S knows that p only if there is no true proposition d such that, were d added to S's total evidence, S would cease to be justified in believing p — unless d is itself defeated by some further true proposition. This recursive structure is meant to filter out misleading defeaters whilst admitting genuine ones. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a woman believes, with good justification, that her colleague was in Leeds on Tuesday. She once read a reliable newspaper report confirming this, but she has since wholly forgotten having read it. The report is true. However, the newspaper published a retraction the following day — also a true event, though the retraction was itself issued in error and the original report was accurate. Now consider the interplay of defeaters. The retraction, being a true proposition, would undermine the evidential force of the original report were both present in the woman's evidence. The fact that the retraction was mistaken would in turn defeat the retraction. But the woman has forgotten the original report altogether; it no longer figures in her conscious evidential base, yet it once contributed to the formation of her belief and may still sustain it in some dispositional sense. The recursive machinery of the definition presupposes that all relevant evidence is available for explicit rational assessment — that defeaters and defeaters-of-defeaters can be weighed against one another in the tribunal of the subject's total evidence. But when evidence has been forgotten yet remains operative in sustaining belief, the tidy hierarchy of defeat upon defeat ceases to function as the analysis requires. The background cases were ones in which the subject's evidence was transparent and fully accessible; the definition, abstracted from such cases, inherited an assumption of evidential tidiness that does not obtain in the epistemically cluttered circumstances of ordinary cognitive life.

background_cases: A student studies a textbook carefully and comes to believe that water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure; were this false, the textbook would have said otherwise, and the student would not have formed the belief.

A birdwatcher sees a flamingo at a lake and believes there is a flamingo at the lake; had there been no flamingo, she would not have formed this belief, for there would have been nothing of that distinctive appearance to trigger it.

A stockbroker reads a reliable financial report and comes to believe that a certain company's share price has risen; had the price not risen, the report would have said so, and the stockbroker would have believed otherwise.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief is sensitive to the truth of p — that is, were p false, S would not believe that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
Were p false, S would not believe that p
counterexample: Consider a person who believes that she is not a brain in a vat being fed artificially generated experiences by a supercomputer. This belief is, let us suppose, true. Yet it is not sensitive: were she a brain in a vat, she would still believe that she is not, for the entire point of the sceptical scenario is that it is experientially indistinguishable from ordinary life. On the sensitivity account, she does not know that she is not a brain in a vat. Yet this seems an extraordinary concession, for it is precisely the sort of proposition whose truth we ordinarily take ourselves to know — or at the very least, whose denial would undermine virtually all of our other knowledge claims.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved beliefs about specific, empirically discriminable states of affairs — the boiling point of water, the presence of a flamingo — where the counterfactual test was tractable. The definition generalised this counterfactual condition to all beliefs, but it cannot accommodate propositions that are true across all epistemically accessible possible worlds, such as the denial of radical sceptical hypotheses.
passage:
It is natural to suppose that a knower is someone who tracks the truth — whose belief would have been different had the facts been otherwise. A student who studies a reliable textbook and learns that water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard pressure would not have formed this belief had it been false, for the textbook would have stated otherwise. A birdwatcher who spots a flamingo at a lake would not have believed a flamingo to be present had there been none, for there would have been nothing of that distinctive colour and form to catch her eye. A stockbroker who reads a sound financial report and believes that a company's share price has risen would have believed otherwise had the price fallen, for the report would have reflected the true state of the market. In each case, the subject's belief varies with the facts: were the facts different, so too would be the belief.

From such cases one might extract a sensitivity condition: S knows that p only if, were p false, S would not believe that p. This counterfactual analysis gives precise expression to the intuitive thought that knowledge involves a responsiveness to how things stand. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a person who believes that she is not a brain in a vat — not, that is, a disembodied brain sustained in a laboratory and fed artificially generated experiences by a supercomputer. Let us grant that this belief is true. It is, however, conspicuously insensitive: were she a brain in a vat, she would still believe that she is not, for the defining feature of the sceptical scenario is its experiential indistinguishability from reality. The sensitivity condition therefore entails that she does not know she is not a brain in a vat. Yet this is a proposition whose truth we should ordinarily take ourselves to be entitled to affirm; its denial would unravel the fabric of virtually all our empirical knowledge. The background cases from which the sensitivity condition was extracted all concerned beliefs about specific, empirically discriminable states of affairs — the boiling point of a substance, the presence of a bird at a lake — where the counterfactual test could be applied without embarrassment. The definition, in generalising this condition to all propositions, overlooked the fact that certain truths — particularly the denials of sceptical hypotheses — hold across all epistemically relevant possible worlds and are therefore not the sort of propositions to which a counterfactual tracking condition can sensibly be applied. What seemed a virtue of the analysis in local cases becomes a liability when extended to the most fundamental commitments of our epistemic framework.

background_cases: A mathematician contemplates the proposition that seven plus five equals twelve and finds it utterly impossible to doubt; she is certain, and she knows.
A person experiencing a sharp pain in his left hand is certain that he is in pain; there is no possibility of error, and he knows that he is in pain.
A logician considers the law of non-contradiction and finds that she cannot coherently conceive of its falsehood; she is certain of it, and she knows it to be true.
definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S is certain that p — that is, S's confidence in p is maximal and admits of no rational doubt.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is certain that p, admitting of no rational doubt
counterexample: A civil engineer knows that the bridge she has designed will support the loads specified in the building code. She has performed extensive calculations, employed well-tested models, used conservative safety factors, and her design has been independently reviewed. Yet she is not certain in the philosophical sense: she can conceive of scenarios — unprecedented material failures, errors in the models, extraordinary loads — in which the bridge might fail. Her confidence is very high but not maximal; rational doubt, however slight, remains.
abductive_insight: The background cases — arithmetic truths, the experience of pain, logical laws — are all instances where the subject-matter is such as to exclude the very possibility of error. The definition generalised the certainty found in these special cases to all knowledge, thereby overlooking that the vast majority of what we ordinarily and rightly claim to know is held with high but not maximal confidence.
passage:
There are propositions of which one may be said to be altogether certain, and which one may be said, with equal confidence, to know. A mathematician who contemplates the sum of seven and five finds the result — twelve — to be beyond the reach of doubt; she cannot coherently suppose it to be otherwise, and we say without hesitation that she knows it. A person who is experiencing a sharp pain in his left hand is certain that he is in pain; there is no gap between the experience and his awareness of it through which error might creep. A logician who considers the law of non-contradiction — that no proposition can be both true and false — finds it to be a condition of thought itself, not a contingent fact that might have been otherwise. In these cases certainty and knowledge march together, and one might be tempted to suppose that they are inseparable.

Accordingly, one might propose that knowledge requires certainty: S knows that p only if S's confidence in p is maximal, admitting of no rational ground for doubt. This analysis has a distinguished pedigree and captures something of importance about the cases just considered. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, or at the very least its incompatibility with ordinary epistemic practice. Consider a civil engineer who has designed a bridge to support the loads specified in the building code. She has performed extensive calculations using well-established structural models; she has employed conservative safety factors; her design has been independently reviewed by competent colleagues. We should say, and she herself would say, that she knows the bridge will bear the specified loads. Yet she is not certain in the sense that the mathematician is certain that seven and five are twelve. She can conceive, without any incoherence, of scenarios in which the bridge might fail — an unprecedented flaw in the steel, an undetected error in the computational model, a load combination not foreseen by the code. Her confidence, though very high and well-founded, is not maximal; a residue of rational doubt, however slight, remains. If knowledge requires certainty of the kind exhibited in the mathematical and logical cases, then the engineer does not know that her bridge will hold — and neither do we know most of the things we ordinarily and rightly claim to know. The background cases from which the certainty requirement was extracted were cases of a very special kind: arithmetic, immediate experience, logical law. These are domains in which the subject-matter itself excludes the possibility of error. The definition, in generalising the certainty found in such privileged domains to all knowledge whatsoever, confused a feature of certain extraordinary epistemic achievements with a condition upon knowledge as such, and thereby threatened to make knowledge a far rarer commodity than our ordinary practice of ascribing it would suggest.

background_cases: A subject perceives a red patch directly before her and forms the belief that something red is present, without inference from any prior belief.
A subject feels a sharp pain in his left hand and thereby believes himself to be in pain, requiring no further justificatory support.
A subject introspects and finds herself experiencing anxiety, forming the belief that she is anxious on no evidential basis beyond the experience itself.
definition: A belief is basically justified if and only if (i) it is not inferred from any other belief, (ii) it arises directly from sensory experience or introspection, and (iii) it concerns the subject's own current mental states or immediate sensory presentations.
conditions: The belief is not inferred from any other belief.
The belief arises directly from sensory experience or introspection.
The belief concerns the subject's own current mental states or immediate sensory presentations.
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who, upon viewing a complex geometrical diagram, immediately and non-inferentially grasps that a certain theorem holds. Her belief is not about her own mental states or sensory presentations, yet it possesses a degree of epistemic immediacy that resists classification as merely derived.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all reflexive or first-personal, which led the analysis to restrict basic justification to beliefs about one's own mental life. But immediacy of justification may attach to beliefs about objective subject-matters when the cognitive relation is suitably direct, a possibility the paradigm cases obscured.
passage:
We may begin by considering several familiar instances in which a belief appears to possess justification of the most fundamental sort. When one perceives a red patch directly before one, the belief that something red is present seems to require no inferential support from further beliefs; it is, as it were, self-standing. Similarly, when one feels a sharp pain and thereupon believes oneself to be in pain, or when one introspects and discovers that one is anxious, these beliefs appear to be justified immediately, without mediation by any propositional intermediary. From such paradigm cases one might extract the following analysis: a belief is basically justified if and only if it is not inferred from any other belief, arises directly from sensory experience or introspection, and concerns the subject's own current mental states or immediate sensory presentations.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a mathematician, well practised in her discipline, examines a complex geometrical diagram and immediately grasps, without conscious inferential steps, that a certain theorem must hold. Her belief is not about her own mental states; it concerns an objective mathematical relation. Nor does it rest upon inference from other beliefs she could articulate, though doubtless her extensive training has disposed her cognitive faculties in ways that make such immediate apprehension possible. Nevertheless, the belief seems to enjoy a species of non-inferential justification that the foregoing analysis cannot accommodate, since the third condition restricts basicality to beliefs about one's own phenomenal life. The difficulty, we may suggest, is that the original cases all shared a reflexive or first-personal character which, though common to each instance, was not in fact essential to the immediacy that rendered them basic. What mattered was the directness of the cognitive relation between the subject and the object of belief, not the restriction of that object to the subject's own mental economy. In abstracting the definition from cases that happened to be introspective or sensory, one inadvertently elevated a contingent feature to the status of a necessary condition, thereby excluding modes of immediate apprehension that are, by any reasonable standard, no less epistemically fundamental.

background_cases: A detective assembles testimony from several witnesses, physical evidence from the scene, and forensic reports, forming a belief about the culprit that is justified because each piece of evidence supports and is supported by the others.
A historian reconciles primary documents, archaeological findings, and contemporaneous correspondence into a coherent account of a medieval battle, each element lending credibility to the rest.

A physician arrives at a diagnosis by integrating the patient's symptoms, laboratory results, and family history into a unified clinical picture in which every datum coheres with the proposed ailment.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) it coheres with the other beliefs in the subject's doxastic system, (ii) the beliefs in that system mutually support one another, and (iii) no belief in the system is logically inconsistent with any other.
conditions: The belief coheres with the other beliefs in the subject's doxastic system.
The beliefs in the system mutually support one another.
No belief in the system is logically inconsistent with any other.
counterexample: Imagine a subject who, through no fault of her own, has constructed an elaborate and internally coherent system of beliefs about a fictional country — its geography, customs, political history, and economy — derived entirely from a novel she mistook for a work of non-fiction. Every belief supports every other; none is inconsistent with any member of the set. Yet the entire system floats free of reality.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved systems that were anchored to the external world through perception, testimony, or empirical investigation. The definition captured the internal relations of coherence but failed to require any external grounding, because such grounding was a shared but unnoticed feature of the paradigm cases.
passage:
It is natural to observe that in many instances of well-justified belief, what underwrites the subject's epistemic position is not a single foundational item but rather the mutual support amongst a body of beliefs. The detective who assembles witness testimony, physical evidence, and forensic analysis into a unified account of the crime enjoys justification precisely because these elements cohere with and reinforce one another. The historian who reconciles documents, artefacts, and correspondence into a narrative of some past event is similarly justified in virtue of the mutual fit amongst the components of her account. And the physician who integrates symptoms, test results, and family history into a diagnosis relies upon the same structure of reciprocal support. From these cases one might propose that a belief is justified provided it coheres with the subject's other beliefs, these beliefs mutually support one another, and no member of the system is logically inconsistent with any other.

However, there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject has, through an innocent error, mistaken an elaborate work of fiction for a factual treatise on a distant country. She forms a rich and detailed system of beliefs about this country's geography, customs, political arrangements, and economy. Every belief in the system supports and is supported by others; no inconsistency can be found. The three conditions of the analysis are fully satisfied. Yet we should hardly wish to say that her beliefs about this non-existent country are justified, for the entire system, however internally harmonious, bears no relation whatever to the world. The original cases all involved systems that were connected to reality through perception, testimony, or empirical investigation — a feature so ubiquitous that it escaped explicit mention in the analysis. What coherentism, thus formulated, omits is the requirement that the system be appropriately anchored to something beyond itself. Internal harmony, it would seem, is necessary but not sufficient; without external constraint, even the most coherent edifice of belief may amount to nothing more than an elaborate and self-consistent fiction.

background_cases: A juror believes the defendant is guilty because the prosecution has presented extensive forensic evidence, multiple eyewitness testimonies, and a clear motive, all pointing in the same direction.
A scientist believes that a chemical compound is toxic because repeated controlled experiments, peer-reviewed literature, and independent replication all converge on that conclusion.
A navigator believes she is at a certain latitude because her sextant reading, her chronometer, and the position of known stars all yield the same result.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the subject possesses a substantial body of evidence bearing on the proposition believed, (ii) that evidence, taken collectively, supports the proposition more strongly than its negation, and (iii) the subject has no countervailing evidence of comparable strength.
conditions: The subject possesses a substantial body of evidence bearing on the proposition believed.
The evidence, taken collectively, supports the proposition more strongly than its negation.
The subject has no countervailing evidence of comparable strength.
counterexample: Consider a subject who possesses overwhelming evidence — drawn from newspaper reports, government statistics, and the testimony of acquaintances — that a particular district of the city is dangerous. Her evidence is abundant and univocal, and she has no countervailing evidence. Yet the evidence is uniformly misleading: a systematic reporting bias has grossly distorted the facts, and the district is in truth quite safe.
abductive_insight: In the background cases, the evidence was not merely plentiful and convergent but also generated by processes that were in fact tracking the truth. The analysis captured the quantity and direction of evidence but overlooked the question of whether the evidential sources were themselves reliable, a feature that the paradigm cases shared but did not make salient.
passage:
There are numerous instances in which we should all agree that a subject's belief is well justified by her evidence. The juror who, having attended to forensic results, eyewitness testimony, and a demonstrated motive, believes the defendant to be guilty, seems justified in proportion to the convergence and weight of that evidence. The scientist whose belief in the toxicity of a compound rests upon repeated experiments, the published literature, and independent replications is similarly well placed. And the navigator who fixes her position by the agreement of sextant, chronometer, and stellar observation enjoys a justification that would be difficult to impugn. From such cases one might propose that a belief is justified when the subject possesses a substantial body of evidence supporting the proposition more strongly than its negation, and has no countervailing evidence of comparable strength.

Yet a case may be constructed which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject possesses a large and convergent body of evidence — drawn from newspaper reports, official statistics, and the testimony of several acquaintances — to the effect that a particular district of her city is exceedingly dangerous. The evidence is abundant; it uniformly supports the proposition; and she has encountered nothing of comparable force telling against it. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied. Yet suppose, further, that the evidence is systematically misleading: a persistent reporting bias, arising from the sensational character of isolated incidents and the political interests of certain officials, has grossly distorted the true state of affairs, and the district is in reality quite safe. We should, it seems, be reluctant to pronounce her belief justified, notwithstanding the quantity and apparent weight of her evidence. The difficulty is that the paradigm cases all involved evidence generated by processes that were in fact reliably connected to the truth — controlled experiments, direct observation, calibrated instruments — and this feature, though present in every instance, was not captured by the analysis. What the definition records is the internal evidential situation of the subject, but what justification appears additionally to require is some assurance that the evidence is not merely copious but also appropriately sourced.

background_cases: A subject forms a belief about the colour of an object by looking at it in good light, and her visual system, functioning as it normally does, produces a true belief.
A subject remembers where she placed her keys yesterday, and her memory, operating in its customary fashion, delivers a correct recollection.
A subject reasons from two premises she knows to be true to a valid conclusion, and her inferential faculty, working as it typically does, yields a justified belief.
A subject forms a belief about the temperature outside by feeling the air on her skin, and her tactile sense, operating reliably, produces an accurate judgement.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) it is produced by a cognitive process that is in fact reliable, (ii) the process operates in a manner that is normal for processes of that type, and (iii) no equally reliable process available to the subject would have produced a contrary belief.
conditions: The belief is produced by a cognitive process that is in fact reliable.
The process operates in a manner that is normal for processes of that type.
No equally reliable process available to the subject would have produced a contrary belief.
counterexample: A subject forms a belief that there is a barn before her by looking at what is in fact a genuine barn. Her visual system operates normally and reliably. Yet she happens to be driving through a region in which nearly all of the apparent barns are elaborate facades, and she has, by sheer chance, looked at the one real barn. No alternative process available to her would contradict the belief.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved environments in which the reliable process was operating in conditions hospitable to its reliability. The analysis specified the internal character of the process but neglected the contribution of the external environment, which in ordinary cases cooperates with the process but in the barn-facade case conspires against it.
passage:
We may observe that in a wide range of uncontroversial cases, a belief is justified when it issues from a cognitive process that is, as a matter of fact, reliable. Seeing the colour of a nearby object in good light, remembering where one placed one's keys, drawing a valid inference from known premises, or judging the temperature by the sensation of air upon one's skin — in each of these instances, the process in question reliably produces true beliefs when operating normally, and we regard the resulting beliefs as justified. One might accordingly propose that a belief is justified if it is produced by a reliable cognitive process operating in the normal manner, and no equally reliable process available to the subject would have yielded a contrary belief.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, and it has been much discussed in the literature. Suppose a subject is driving through a rural district and, looking to her left, perceives what appears to be a barn. She forms the belief that there is a barn before her, and in this she is quite correct — it is a genuine barn. Her visual system is operating normally and is, in the relevant sense, reliable. No alternative process available to her would produce a conflicting judgement. Yet, unbeknownst to her, the district is populated almost entirely with elaborate barn facades, indistinguishable from real barns at the distance from which she views them. She has, by coincidence, fixed her gaze upon the single genuine article. All three conditions of the analysis are met, yet one feels a considerable reluctance to pronounce her belief justified, for she might so easily have been looking at any of the surrounding facades and would have formed the same belief with equal confidence. The trouble, we may suggest, is that reliability is not a property of the process alone but of the process in its environment. The paradigm cases all involved processes operating in environments that were, so to speak, cooperative — environments in which barns were barns and not facades. The analysis abstracted the reliability of the process without attending to the hospitality of the surroundings, thereby admitting cases in which a process that is reliable in the abstract is rendered epistemically impotent by the peculiarities of the local situation.

background_cases: A subject with normal vision sees a tree in the garden and forms the belief that there is a tree present; her visual faculty is functioning as it was designed to function and produces a true belief in a suitable environment.
A subject with an unimpaired memory recalls the date of a friend's birthday and forms a correct belief; her mnemonic faculty is operating according to its proper function.
A subject with normal reasoning abilities draws a correct conclusion from premises she has good reason to accept; her inferential faculty is functioning as it ought.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) it is produced by a cognitive faculty that is functioning properly (i.e., in accordance with its design plan), (ii) the faculty in question is aimed at the production of true beliefs, and (iii) the design plan is a good one, in the sense that when the faculty functions properly, it is objectively likely to produce true beliefs.
conditions: The belief is produced by a cognitive faculty functioning properly, in accordance with its design plan.
The faculty is aimed at the production of true beliefs.
The design plan is a good one, such that proper functioning is objectively likely to produce true beliefs.
counterexample: Suppose a human subject is transported, without her knowledge, to a planet whose atmosphere subtly refracts light in a manner that makes distant objects appear to be approximately thirty degrees from their actual positions. Her visual faculty is functioning precisely as it was designed to function; it is aimed at truth; and its design plan is an excellent one for terrestrial conditions. Yet nearly every belief she forms about the spatial arrangement of distant objects is false.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved faculties operating in the environment for which they were, in a sense, designed — the environment in which their design plan could be expected to succeed. The analysis specified that the design plan must be a good one but did not require that the faculty be operating in the environment to which the plan is suited, because this condition was silently satisfied in every paradigm case.
passage:
There is a familiar and attractive thought that justification is connected with the proper functioning of our cognitive faculties. When one sees a tree in the garden and thereupon believes that there is a tree present, the justification of this belief seems to depend upon the fact that one's visual faculty is operating as it was designed to operate, in the service of truth, and that the design in question is a sound one. The same may be said of the subject who remembers a friend's birthday or who draws a valid inference from well-supported premises: in each case, a faculty aimed at truth is functioning properly according to a reliable design plan. From such cases one might propose that a belief is justified when it is produced by a properly functioning faculty, aimed at truth, whose design plan is such that proper functioning is objectively likely to yield true beliefs.

But there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Imagine that a human subject is transported, without any awareness on her part, to a distant planet whose atmosphere refracts light in a peculiar fashion, causing distant objects to appear shifted some thirty degrees from their true positions. Her visual system is in perfect working order; it is functioning precisely as it was designed to function. The design plan is an excellent one — it has served the human species admirably for millennia. And the faculty is, without question, aimed at the production of true beliefs about the spatial arrangement of objects. Yet every perceptual belief she forms about the positions of distant things is systematically false. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied, and yet the resulting beliefs are not merely accidentally false but reliably so, which ought to give us pause before pronouncing them justified. The source of the difficulty is that the paradigm cases all involved faculties operating within the environment for which they were, so to speak, fitted. The terrestrial environment cooperates with the human visual system in ways that the alien atmosphere does not. The analysis captured the internal soundness of the design plan but omitted the requirement that the faculty operate in an environment congenial to that plan — a requirement invisibly satisfied in all the cases from which the definition was drawn.

background_cases: A subject believes it will rain because she can see dark clouds, feel the humidity, and recall that the forecast predicted rain; she can, upon reflection, cite each of these reasons.
A subject believes his colleague is upset because he noticed her furrowed brow, curt replies, and avoidance of conversation; he can articulate these observations as grounds for his belief.
A subject believes a mathematical proposition because she has carefully followed the proof and can rehearse each step if asked.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the subject has reasons that support the belief, (ii) the subject is able, upon reflection, to cite or articulate those reasons, and (iii) the reasons the subject can cite are in fact adequate to support the belief.
conditions: The subject has reasons that support the belief.
The subject is able, upon reflection, to cite or articulate those reasons.
The reasons the subject can cite are in fact adequate to support the belief.
counterexample: Consider a seasoned chicken-sexer who reliably sorts day-old chicks by sex with remarkable accuracy. She has a justified belief, on each occasion, that the chick before her is male or female, as the case may be. Yet she is wholly unable to articulate the grounds of her judgement; she cannot say what features she is responding to, and introspection reveals nothing beyond a vague sense of confidence.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved beliefs whose justificatory basis was transparent to the subject. The analysis required that the subject be able to articulate her reasons, but justification may sometimes rest upon sub-personal or inarticulate cognitive processes whose deliverances are no less epistemically respectable for being opaque to introspection.
passage:
In a great many familiar cases, justified belief appears to require that the subject be in a position to say why she holds the belief in question. When one believes it will rain because one has observed dark clouds, felt the humidity, and recalled the weather forecast, one can, if pressed, enumerate these reasons, and it is their availability to reflection that seems partly constitutive of one's justification. Likewise, the subject who believes a colleague is upset can cite the furrowed brow, the curt replies, and the avoidance of conversation; and the mathematician who believes a theorem can rehearse the steps of the proof. From cases of this sort, one might extract the following analysis: a belief is justified if the subject has reasons that support it, is able upon reflection to articulate those reasons, and the articulable reasons are in fact adequate to the support of the belief.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, and it is drawn from a rather humble occupation. The experienced chicken-sexer, whose task it is to sort day-old chicks by sex, performs this feat with an accuracy that far exceeds chance and that compares favourably with more elaborate methods of determination. On each occasion, she forms a belief — that the chick before her is male, or that it is female — and this belief is, by all ordinary standards, justified: it is reliably produced, practically indispensable, and overwhelmingly likely to be true. Yet she is quite unable to articulate the basis of her judgement. Introspection reveals nothing beyond a vague feeling of confidence; she cannot specify what perceptual features she is responding to, nor reconstruct any chain of reasoning. The second condition of the analysis — that the subject be able, upon reflection, to cite her reasons — is plainly unsatisfied. The paradigm cases from which the analysis was drawn all involved beliefs whose justificatory grounds were, as it were, luminous to the subject. But the chicken-sexer reminds us that cognitive processes may operate below the threshold of introspective access, delivering beliefs that are no less justified for being opaque in their origins. The demand for articulability, whilst natural enough when one's examples are drawn from everyday deliberation, proves too restrictive when extended to the full range of epistemic achievement.

background_cases: A subject diligently consults multiple reputable sources before forming a belief about the safety of a medication, thereby fulfilling her epistemic duty to investigate before believing.
A subject, aware of his tendency toward confirmation bias, actively seeks out evidence against his favoured hypothesis before drawing a conclusion, thereby fulfilling his duty of impartiality.
A subject suspends judgement on a matter about which she recognises herself to be ignorant, thereby fulfilling her duty not to believe beyond the evidence.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the subject has fulfilled all relevant epistemic duties in forming the belief, (ii) the subject has not been epistemically negligent or reckless, and (iii) no further epistemic duty, the fulfilment of which was within the subject's power, would have led their to revise the belief.
conditions: The subject has fulfilled all relevant epistemic duties in forming the belief.
The subject has not been epistemically negligent or reckless.
No further epistemic duty, the fulfilment of which was within the subject's power, would have led the subject to revise the belief.
counterexample: Suppose a subject living in a community in which a certain falsehood is universally accepted consults every source available to her, reflects carefully, considers objections, and forms her belief with the utmost conscientiousness. She has discharged every epistemic duty one could reasonably impose. Yet the belief is false and, moreover, is supported by nothing beyond the pervasive but unfounded consensus of her community. She is, one might say, blamelessly but unjustifiedly believing.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved subjects whose epistemic environment contained sources that were, in fact, connected to the truth. The analysis specified the subject's duties but not the quality of the epistemic environment, because in the paradigm cases a hospitable environment was present but unremarked.
passage:
There is a venerable tradition in epistemology according to which justification is fundamentally a deontological notion — that to be justified is to have fulfilled one's epistemic duties. The subject who consults reputable sources before forming a belief about the safety of a medication, the enquirer who actively seeks countervailing evidence against his favoured hypothesis, and the person who suspends judgement where she recognises her ignorance — each of these exemplifies epistemic conscientiousness, and we are disposed to regard their beliefs, or their withholdings, as justified in consequence. From such cases one might propose that a belief is justified when the subject has fulfilled all relevant epistemic duties, has not been negligent or reckless, and no further feasible exercise of duty would have led her to revise the belief.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us suppose a subject lives in a small and isolated community in which a certain falsehood — say, that a particular herbal preparation cures a grave illness — is universally accepted. She consults every source available to her: the community's healers, its elders, its written records. She reflects with care, considers what objections she can think of, and finds that all of her evidence points in one direction. She is neither negligent nor reckless; indeed, she has been a model of epistemic conscientiousness. No further exercise of duty within her power would have altered her conclusion, for the falsehood is so deeply embedded in her community that every available avenue of enquiry confirms it. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Yet the belief is false, and what supported it was not genuine evidence but merely the echo of a collective error. We should, one feels, hesitate to call her belief justified, even as we acknowledge that she is entirely without blame. The paradigm cases all involved subjects whose epistemic environments contained sources that were in fact connected to the truth — reputable medical literature, genuine counterevidence, recognisable limits of knowledge. The analysis abstracted the subject's conscientious performance of her duties but failed to notice that, in every case from which it was drawn, the environment cooperated by furnishing materials adequate to the task. Deontological fidelity, it appears, is not sufficient for justification when the environment is too impoverished to reward even the most scrupulous enquiry.

background_cases: A detective believes the butler committed the crime because the butler's fingerprints were found on the weapon, and it is this evidence that causally sustains her belief.
A student believes the earth orbits the sun because she has studied the astronomical evidence, and it is this evidence that is causally responsible for her belief.
A doctor believes the patient has influenza because the symptoms and test results point to that diagnosis, and it is these clinical data that causally ground his belief.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the subject possesses adequate evidence for the belief, (ii) the belief is causally based on that evidence, and (iii) the evidence is of a kind that would lead a reasonable person to hold the belief.
conditions: The subject possesses adequate evidence for the belief.
The belief is causally based on that evidence.
The evidence is of a kind that would lead a reasonable person to hold the belief.
counterexample: Suppose a subject possesses excellent evidence that her business partner is embezzling funds — she has found discrepancies in the accounts, suspicious transfers, and a hidden ledger. However, she does not form her belief about the embezzlement on the basis of this evidence. Instead, she forms it because of an irrational prejudice against her partner, and only later discovers the accounting evidence, which she then possesses but which plays no causal role in sustaining the belief that was already fixed by prejudice.
abductive_insight: The background cases all featured a straightforward causal path from evidence to belief. The analysis required that the belief be causally based on the evidence, but it did not specify the manner or route of the causal connection, thereby admitting cases in which the evidence is present and the belief is caused, but the causal path is epistemically defective.
passage:
It is widely acknowledged that for a belief to be justified, it is not enough that the subject merely possess good evidence; the belief must also be properly based upon that evidence. The detective who believes the butler guilty because of the fingerprints on the weapon, the student who believes the earth orbits the sun because of the astronomical evidence she has studied, and the physician who diagnoses influenza on the strength of symptoms and laboratory results — in each of these cases, the evidence is not merely present in the subject's cognitive economy but is causally responsible for the belief. One might accordingly propose that a belief is justified when the subject possesses adequate evidence, the belief is causally based upon it, and the evidence is of a sort that would lead a reasonable person to the same conclusion.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, at least as stated. Suppose a subject — a businesswoman — possesses compelling evidence that her partner is embezzling: discrepancies in the accounts, suspicious transfers, a concealed ledger. She does indeed believe that her partner is embezzling. But the causal history of her belief is not what one might expect. She formed the belief initially out of an irrational personal animus toward her partner, well before she encountered any of the accounting irregularities. The evidence was discovered afterwards and now sits, as it were, alongside the belief without having caused or even reinforced it; the belief was already firmly fixed by prejudice. In a certain sense, conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied: she possesses adequate evidence, and the evidence is of a kind that would persuade a reasonable person. Condition (ii) — that the belief be causally based on the evidence — may appear to present an obstacle, yet the difficulty is subtler than a simple failure of causation. The analysis specifies that the belief must be causally based on the evidence but says nothing about the character of the causal route. In the paradigm cases, the causal connection was epistemically appropriate — evidence was gathered, weighed, and issued in belief through a rational process. The analysis, having been drawn from such well-behaved cases, failed to distinguish between causal basing that proceeds through epistemically respectable channels and causal histories that are, from the standpoint of justification, defective. What is wanted is not merely a causal link between evidence and belief, but the right kind of causal link.

background_cases: A subject forms true beliefs about her surroundings by means of ordinary visual perception, a process that is both truth-conducive and, we should say, confers justification on the resulting beliefs.
A subject arrives at true beliefs about arithmetical sums by means of careful calculation, a process that reliably leads to truth and that we regard as justifying.

A subject forms true beliefs about others' emotional states by attentive observation of facial expressions and tone of voice, a process that tends toward truth and that we take to confer justification.
definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) it is produced by a process that is truth-conducive, (ii) the process in question would produce a high proportion of true beliefs across a wide range of relevantly similar circumstances, and (iii) the subject has no reason to believe the process is unreliable.
conditions: The belief is produced by a truth-conducive process.
The process would produce a high proportion of true beliefs across a wide range of relevantly similar circumstances.
The subject has no reason to believe the process is unreliable.
counterexample: Suppose a subject is fitted, without her knowledge, with a device that stimulates her brain so as to produce beliefs about the stock prices of various companies, and these beliefs are invariably correct. The process is perfectly truth-conducive; it would yield true beliefs across a wide range of circumstances; and the subject has no reason to suspect its unreliability. Yet the beliefs seem to lack justification, for the subject has no understanding of how she comes to hold them, and the process bypasses her rational agency entirely.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved processes that were not merely truth-conducive but also integrated into the subject's rational cognitive life — processes she could, in principle, monitor, evaluate, and correct. The analysis captured truth-conduciveness but omitted the requirement that the process be appropriately connected to the subject's rational agency, a feature that the paradigm cases possessed without exception.
passage:
It is tempting to suppose that justification is, at bottom, a matter of truth-conduciveness — that a belief is justified when it is produced by a process that tends to deliver truths. Ordinary visual perception, careful arithmetical calculation, and the attentive reading of others' emotional expressions by means of facial cues and vocal tone are all processes that, in their several ways, reliably produce true beliefs; and we do, as a matter of course, regard beliefs so produced as justified. From such cases one might extract the analysis that a belief is justified if it is produced by a truth-conducive process, the process would yield a high proportion of true beliefs across a wide range of relevantly similar circumstances, and the subject has no reason to believe it unreliable.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject has been fitted — entirely without her knowledge or consent — with a cerebral device that stimulates her brain in such a manner as to produce beliefs about the stock prices of various companies. These beliefs are, as it happens, invariably correct.
