The process is, by any standard, truth-conducive; it would produce true beliefs across an enormous range of circumstances; and the subject, being unaware of the device, has no reason whatever to doubt its reliability. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one feels a strong reluctance to pronounce these beliefs justified. The subject has no understanding of how or why she holds them; they arrive unbidden and without any connection to her deliberative life. She cannot evaluate the process, monitor its deliverances, or correct it when it goes astray, for she does not even know it exists. The background cases — perception, calculation, the reading of expressions — all involved processes that were integrated into the subject's rational cognitive economy: processes she could attend to, reflect upon, and in principle criticise. Truth-conduciveness, whilst undoubtedly a desideratum, appears insufficient to confer justification when the process that produces the belief is entirely alien to the subject's rational agency. What the paradigm cases shared, but the analysis failed to record, was that the truth-conducive processes in question were also processes over which the subject exercised a measure of rational oversight — a feature that the cerebral implant conspicuously lacks.

background_cases: A person who sincerely asserts that it is raining and acts accordingly — carrying an umbrella, warning others — is properly said to believe it is raining.
A woman who affirms in conversation that the train departs at noon and arranges her schedule on this basis is properly said to believe the train departs at noon.
A child who tells his mother that the dog is in the garden, and refuses to go outside for fear of the animal, is properly said to believe the dog is in the garden.
definition: S believes that p if and only if (i) S sincerely asserts p, (ii) S acts in ways consonant with the truth of p, and (iii) S would avow p upon reflection.
conditions: sincerely asserts p
acts in ways consonant with truth of p
would avow p upon reflection
counterexample: A heavy smoker sincerely asserts that smoking causes cancer, acts in various ways consonant with this truth — he does not encourage others to smoke, he supports anti-smoking legislation — and upon reflection he avows the proposition without hesitation. All three conditions are met. Yet he continues to smoke, an action radically inconsonant with the proposition. His behaviour is partially consonant and partially at odds with p, and one might doubt whether he fully believes it. The case reveals a fragmentation in his doxastic state which the definition cannot accommodate.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved subjects whose behaviour was uniformly consonant with the believed proposition. The definition generalised from these harmonious cases but failed to require that consonance be comprehensive rather than merely partial, and so it cannot detect the fragmented belief states characteristic of weakness of will.
passage:
Consider the following cases. A person who sincerely asserts that it is raining and acts accordingly — carrying an umbrella, warning others — is properly said to believe it is raining. A woman who affirms that the train departs at noon and arranges her schedule on this basis is said to believe this. A child who tells his mother that the dog is in the garden, and refuses to venture outside for fear of the animal, is said to believe the dog is there. From such cases one might extract the following definition: S believes that p if and only if S sincerely asserts p, S acts in ways consonant with the truth of p, and S would avow p upon reflection.

Yet this analysis, for all its plausibility, may be challenged by a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a heavy smoker who sincerely asserts that smoking causes cancer. He supports anti-smoking legislation, counsels his children against the habit, and upon reflection avows the proposition without equivocation. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet he continues to smoke — an action strikingly at odds with the belief he professes. His behaviour is partially consonant with the truth of p and partially in flagrant contradiction of it. Does he fully believe that smoking causes cancer? The matter is far from clear. The background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved subjects whose actions were uniformly and comprehensively consonant with the propositions they asserted. There was no discrepancy between word and deed. The definition, generalising from such harmonious cases, failed to require that the consonance of action be comprehensive rather than merely partial. It thereby admits cases of fragmented or compartmentalised belief — cases in which the subject asserts p and acts upon it in some domains whilst ignoring it in others — and cannot distinguish these from the wholehearted belief exemplified in the background cases.

background_cases: A scientist who revises her belief about the age of a fossil upon discovering new radiocarbon data is engaged in rational belief revision.
A historian who abandons a thesis about the cause of a war when a newly uncovered document contradicts it is engaged in rational belief revision.
A physician who changes his diagnosis when a blood test returns unexpected results is engaged in rational belief revision.
definition: S rationally revises their belief set in response to new evidence e if and only if (i) S incorporates e into their belief set, (ii) S removes any beliefs inconsistent with e, and (iii) S retains all beliefs consistent with e.
conditions: incorporates e into belief set
removes beliefs inconsistent with e
retains all beliefs consistent with e
counterexample: A detective receives new evidence — a reliable alibi for the suspect — and incorporates it into her belief set, removing the belief that the suspect is guilty (which is inconsistent with the alibi) and retaining all beliefs consistent with the alibi. All three conditions are met. But among the retained beliefs is a complex conspiracy theory which, though logically consistent with the alibi, was originally adopted only because it supported the now-abandoned hypothesis of the suspect's guilt. Rationality would seem to require that this theory, having lost its only evidential support, be abandoned as well. Yet the definition, requiring only the retention of beliefs consistent with the new evidence, demands its retention.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved belief sets in which the retained beliefs had independent evidential support. The definition, generalising from these well-ordered cases, required only logical consistency for retention, and thereby failed to capture the further rational requirement that retained beliefs possess independent evidential warrant.
passage:
Consider the following cases of rational belief revision. A scientist revises her view of a fossil's age upon receiving new radiocarbon data. A historian abandons a thesis about the cause of a war when a newly discovered document contradicts it. A physician changes his diagnosis when blood tests return unexpected results. In each case, the subject incorporates the new evidence, removes beliefs inconsistent with it, and retains the remainder. From such cases one might extract the definition: S rationally revises her belief set in response to new evidence e if and only if S incorporates e, removes beliefs inconsistent with e, and retains all beliefs consistent with e.

Yet this analysis proves inadequate. Suppose a detective who has long believed a certain suspect to be guilty of a crime. In support of this belief she has constructed an elaborate theory involving a conspiracy among the suspect's associates. She now receives a decisive piece of new evidence — a reliable alibi establishing the suspect's innocence. She incorporates the alibi, removes the belief in the suspect's guilt, and retains all beliefs consistent with the alibi. The conspiracy theory is logically consistent with the alibi — it does not entail the suspect's guilt — and so the definition requires its retention. Yet the conspiracy theory was adopted solely because it supported the hypothesis of guilt. With that hypothesis removed, the theory has lost its only evidential raison d'être. Rationality, one should think, requires its abandonment. The background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved belief sets in which the retained beliefs enjoyed independent evidential support; none involved beliefs which were parasitic upon the abandoned hypothesis. The definition, by requiring only logical consistency for retention, failed to capture the further rational requirement that retained beliefs possess support of their own.

background_cases: A juror who believes the defendant is guilty because the evidence presented at trial strongly supports this conclusion holds a rational belief.
A student who believes that Caesar crossed the Rubicon because her history textbook says so and she has no reason to doubt it holds a rational belief.
A pedestrian who believes it will rain because he sees dark clouds gathering holds a rational belief.
definition: S's belief that p is rational if and only if (i) S has adequate evidence for p, (ii) S has formed the belief through a process of weighing the evidence, and (iii) S is not ignoring any readily available counter-evidence.
conditions: has adequate evidence for p
formed belief through weighing evidence
not ignoring readily available counter-evidence
counterexample: A philosopher, after decades of careful study, weighs the evidence for and against the existence of God with exemplary thoroughness. She does not ignore any counter-evidence. She concludes that the evidence is exactly balanced — it supports neither theism nor atheism. She then forms the belief that God exists on the basis of a Pascalian calculation about the practical benefits of belief. She has adequate evidence (in the sense that the evidence is not against p), she has weighed the evidence, and she ignores no counter-evidence. Yet her belief is formed not by the evidential weighing but by a practical calculation. The three conditions are met, but the belief's causal basis is prudential rather than evidential, and many would deny its rationality.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved subjects whose beliefs were not only supported by evidence but were formed because of the evidence. The definition captured the presence of evidence and the process of weighing but failed to require that the belief be formed in virtue of the evidential weighing rather than for extraneous practical reasons.
passage:
Consider the following cases of rational belief. A juror believes the defendant guilty because the trial evidence strongly supports this conclusion. A student believes that Caesar crossed the Rubicon because her textbook reports it and she has no reason to doubt the source. A pedestrian believes it will rain because he sees dark clouds gathering. In each case, the subject has adequate evidence, has weighed it with due care, and ignores no available counter-evidence. From these cases one might extract the definition: S's belief that p is rational if and only if S has adequate evidence, has formed the belief by weighing the evidence, and is not ignoring any readily available counter-evidence.

This analysis may, however, be challenged. Suppose a philosopher who, after decades of careful study, has weighed the evidence for and against the existence of God with exemplary thoroughness, ignoring no counter-evidence, and has concluded that the evidence is exactly balanced. It supports neither theism nor atheism. She then forms the belief that God exists, motivated not by the evidence — which, as she acknowledges, is neutral — but by a Pascalian calculation that the practical benefits of belief outweigh the costs of disbelief. She has adequate evidence, in the minimal sense that the evidence does not tell against her belief. She has weighed the evidence. She ignores no counter-evidence. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet her belief is formed not because of the evidential weighing but because of a prudential calculation extraneous to the evidence. The background cases all involved subjects whose beliefs were formed in virtue of the evidence; the definition, generalising from these, required only the presence of evidence and the process of weighing, but failed to require that the belief be causally produced by the evidential assessment rather than by a distinct, non-epistemic motive.

background_cases: A person who has been taught that the earth is round and has never encountered any reason to doubt this may be said to hold the belief dispositionally — he would affirm it if asked, but he does not presently entertain it.

A woman who learned her multiplication tables in childhood and can still produce the answer to 7 times 8, though she has not thought about it in years, may be said to believe dispositionally that 7 times 8 is 56.

A driver who knows the route to his office and would describe it accurately if asked, though he drives it without conscious thought, may be said to believe dispositionally that the route runs along certain streets.
definition: S has a dispositional belief that p if and only if (i) S has at some point entertained p, (ii) S has not subsequently rejected p, and (iii) S would affirm p if the question were raised under normal conditions.
conditions: has at some point entertained p
has not subsequently rejected p
would affirm p if question raised under normal conditions
counterexample: Consider a person who has never entertained the proposition that his grandmother's maiden name was not 'Napoleon.' He has never thought about this — the question has simply never arisen. Yet if asked, he would unhesitatingly affirm it; he knows his grandmother's name and it is plainly not Napoleon. The first condition — that he has at some point entertained p — is not met, for the proposition has never crossed his mind. Yet one might wish to say that he dispositionally believes it. The definition, by requiring prior entertainment, excludes a vast class of propositions which the subject has never considered but would immediately affirm.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved propositions which the subject had at some point consciously entertained and retained. The definition generalised from these cases of stored-and-retrieved belief but failed to accommodate the class of dispositional beliefs which have never been explicitly formulated — beliefs which are, as it were, available without having been previously activated.
passage:
Consider the following cases of dispositional belief. A person who was taught that the earth is round holds this belief dispositionally: he would affirm it if asked, though he does not presently entertain it. A woman who learned her multiplication tables in childhood believes dispositionally that seven times eight is fifty-six. A driver who knows his route to the office believes dispositionally that it runs along certain streets. In each case, the subject has at some point entertained the proposition, has not subsequently rejected it, and would affirm it if asked. From these cases one might extract the definition: S has a dispositional belief that p if and only if S has at some point entertained p, has not rejected p, and would affirm p if the question were raised under normal conditions.

Yet this analysis proves too restrictive. Consider a person who has never in his life entertained the proposition that his grandmother's maiden name was not 'Napoleon.' The question has simply never arisen. He has never thought about it, and so the first condition — prior entertainment of p — is not satisfied. Yet if someone were to ask him whether his grandmother's maiden name was Napoleon, he would unhesitatingly deny it, for he knows her name and it is plainly otherwise. It seems natural to say that he dispositionally believes the proposition in question, notwithstanding that he has never entertained it. The background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved propositions which had been consciously considered at some point and stored for later retrieval. The definition generalised from these paradigms of explicit-then-dispositional belief but failed to accommodate a different species of dispositional belief — beliefs which have never been explicitly formulated but which the subject would immediately affirm on the basis of other things he knows. The definition, by requiring prior entertainment, draws the boundary of dispositional belief too narrowly.

background_cases: A jury finds a defendant guilty: each juror individually believes him guilty, and the collective body pronounces guilt. The group belief aligns with the individual beliefs.
A faculty committee concludes that a candidate is qualified for tenure: each member individually believes this, and the committee's official position reflects the consensus.
A family decides that the cottage in Wales is the best holiday destination: each member prefers it, and the family as a whole adopts this view.
definition: A group G believes that p if and only if (i) each member of G individually believes that p, (ii) the members are mutually aware that each believes p, and (iii) the group has adopted p as its official position.
conditions: each member individually believes p
mutual awareness of shared belief
group has adopted p as official position
counterexample: Consider a government cabinet which, following a contested vote, adopts as its official position that a certain economic policy is sound. The vote was three to two. Two ministers believe the policy is unsound. Yet by the convention of cabinet collective responsibility, the cabinet presents a united front: all five ministers publicly defend the policy, and the dissenting two, bound by the convention, do not voice their disagreement. Condition (iii) is met — the group has adopted the position officially. The members are mutually aware of the vote and hence of each other's true views — condition (ii) is in a sense met. But condition (i) fails — not every member individually believes p. Yet the cabinet, as a group, believes the policy is sound in the politically operative sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved groups in which unanimity obtained: every member individually believed the proposition, and the group's official position was a natural expression of this unanimity. The definition, generalising from unanimous cases, required individual belief from every member, and so could not accommodate groups which acquire beliefs through majoritarian or procedural mechanisms that override individual dissent.
passage:
Consider the following cases of group belief. A jury finds a defendant guilty, and each juror individually believes him so; the collective verdict aligns with the individual convictions. A faculty committee concludes that a candidate merits tenure, and each member shares this view. A family collectively decides that the Welsh cottage is the best holiday destination, each member preferring it. From such cases one might extract the definition: a group G believes that p if and only if each member individually believes p, the members are mutually aware that each believes p, and the group has adopted p as its official position.

This analysis, however natural in the cases from which it was drawn, proves too restrictive. Consider a government cabinet which, following a contested vote of three to two, adopts as its official position that a particular economic policy is sound. Two of the five ministers believe the policy to be unsound. By the convention of cabinet collective responsibility, however, all five ministers publicly defend the policy, and the dissenters refrain from voicing their disagreement. The group has adopted the position officially; the members are mutually aware of each other's views, having witnessed the vote. But not every member individually believes the policy to be sound; the first condition fails. Yet it seems perfectly natural, and indeed politically essential, to say that the cabinet believes the policy is sound. The cabinet acts upon this belief, defends it in Parliament, and stakes its authority upon it. The background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved groups characterised by unanimity, and the definition, generalising from these harmonious instances, built unanimity into its conditions. It thereby excluded groups which arrive at collective beliefs through majoritarian or procedural mechanisms — mechanisms which may override individual dissent and yet produce genuine group belief in the operative sense of the term.

background_cases: A person who knowingly eats spoiled food, saying he believes it is fresh, is self-deceived: he recognises the evidence of spoilage but persuades himself otherwise.
A student who has received a failing mark but tells herself she passed, despite having seen the grade, is self-deceived: the evidence is clear, yet she forms the contrary belief.
A businessman who overlooks manifest signs of his partner's embezzlement, insisting on the partner's honesty, is self-deceived: the evidence is available, yet he forms a belief at odds with it.
definition: S is self-deceived about p if and only if (i) S has access to evidence strongly supporting not-p, (ii) S forms or maintains the belief that p, and (iii) S's acquisition or maintenance of the belief is intentional — S deliberately brings it about that S believes p.
conditions: access to evidence strongly supporting not-p
forms or maintains belief that p
acquisition of belief is intentional
counterexample: Consider a woman who has access to strong evidence that her marriage is failing — her spouse is distant, uncommunicative, and has retained a solicitor. She forms the belief that her marriage is flourishing. But her belief is not intentionally formed; she does not deliberately persuade herself. Rather, she is psychologically incapable of processing the threatening evidence — it is filtered out by an unconscious defensive mechanism before it reaches the level of deliberate cognition. She is self-deceived, yet her self-deception is not intentional in any ordinary sense. The third condition fails, but the phenomenon is present.
abductive_insight: The background cases, as described, all suggested an element of deliberateness in the self-deceptive process — the subjects appeared to 'persuade themselves' or 'insist' upon their beliefs. The definition, taking this appearance at face value, built intentionality into the analysis. But self-deception may be — and often is — the product of unconscious psychological mechanisms rather than deliberate self-manipulation.
passage:
Consider the following cases of self-deception. A person who knowingly eats spoiled food whilst telling himself it is fresh has, it seems, deliberately brought it about that he believes something contrary to the evidence. A student who has seen her failing mark yet insists she has passed appears to have persuaded herself intentionally. A businessman who overlooks his partner's embezzlement seems to maintain his belief in the partner's honesty through a deliberate act of avoidance. From such cases one might extract the definition: S is self-deceived about p if and only if S has access to evidence strongly supporting not-p, S forms or maintains the belief that p, and S's acquisition or maintenance of the belief is intentional.

Yet this analysis may be challenged by a case which reveals a gap in the intentionality condition. Consider a woman who has access to abundant evidence that her marriage is failing — her spouse has grown distant, has retained a solicitor, and has spoken openly of separation. She nonetheless believes that her marriage is flourishing. But when we examine her psychological state, we find no evidence of intentional self-persuasion. She has not deliberately averted her gaze from the evidence, nor has she constructed rationalisations. The evidence has simply failed to register; an unconscious defensive mechanism filters it out before it reaches deliberate cognition. She is not aware of having formed her belief through any act of will. Yet she is, by any reasonable standard, self-deceived. The background cases, as described, all carried the suggestion of deliberateness: the subjects 'told themselves' or 'insisted.' The definition, taking this surface feature as essential, built intentionality into the analysis. But self-deception, as the present case illustrates, frequently operates beneath the level of intentional action, through unconscious processes which the subject neither initiates nor controls. The definition, by requiring intentionality, excludes what may be the most common and psychologically interesting form of self-deception.

background_cases: A man believes that the cat is on the mat. He can articulate this belief, reason about it, and report it to others. His belief is explicit.
A woman believes that Paris is the capital of France. She learned this years ago and can state it at will. Her belief is explicit.
A child believes that his birthday is in March. He can tell you the date and anticipates the event. His belief is explicit.
definition: S has an explicit belief that p if and only if (i) S has at some point consciously formed the belief that p, (ii) S can articulate p in language, and (iii) S recognises p as something S believes when p is presented to S.
conditions: consciously formed belief at some point
can articulate p in language
recognises p as something believed when presented
counterexample: Consider a pre-linguistic infant who has formed, through repeated experience, the expectation that her mother will appear when she cries. She has consciously formed this expectation — she exhibits anticipatory behaviour — and she recognises the pattern, for she shows distress when the expectation is violated. But she cannot articulate p in language, for she has no language. Condition (ii) fails. Yet one might wish to say her belief is explicit rather than implicit: it is before her mind, it guides her behaviour, and it is not merely a logical consequence of other beliefs. The definition, by requiring linguistic articulation, excludes explicit beliefs held by beings who lack language.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved linguistically competent subjects whose explicit beliefs were expressed in language. The definition, generalising from these cases, treated linguistic articulation as a necessary condition of explicit belief. But explicit belief — belief that is before the mind and actively operative — can obtain in creatures who lack the capacity for linguistic expression.
passage:
Consider the following cases of explicit belief. A man believes that the cat is on the mat; he can state this, reason about it, and report it to others. A woman believes that Paris is the capital of France; she learned it long ago and can express it at will. A child believes that his birthday is in March; he anticipates the event and tells his friends. In each case, the belief has been consciously formed, is linguistically articulable, and is recognised by the subject as something he or she believes. From these cases one might extract the definition: S has an explicit belief that p if and only if S has consciously formed the belief, can articulate p in language, and recognises p as something believed when p is presented.

Yet this analysis encounters a difficulty when we consider subjects who lack language. Imagine a pre-linguistic infant who, through repeated experience, has formed the expectation that her mother will appear when she cries. She has consciously formed this expectation in the sense that it is before her mind and actively guides her behaviour — she exhibits anticipatory movements, turns toward the door, and shows marked distress when the expectation is violated. She recognises the pattern in the sense that she responds differentially when it is confirmed or denied. But she cannot articulate the proposition in language, for she possesses no language. The second condition fails. Yet her belief, if belief it be, seems explicit rather than implicit: it is not merely a logical consequence of other beliefs she holds, but an active, present, operative representation. The background cases from which the definition was extracted all involved linguistically competent adults, and the definition, by generalising from these, made linguistic articulation a necessary condition of explicit belief. In doing so, it excluded the explicit beliefs of pre-linguistic creatures — a restriction which seems to confuse the expression of belief with belief itself.

background_cases: A man asserts 'It is raining' because he believes it is raining; his assertion expresses his belief. We call this a sincere assertion.
A witness testifies that the defendant was at the scene because she believes she saw him there; her testimony expresses her belief. This is a sincere assertion.
A teacher tells her pupils that the earth is approximately spherical because she believes this to be true; her statement expresses her belief. This is a sincere assertion.
definition: S sincerely asserts p if and only if (i) S asserts p, (ii) S believes p, and (iii) S's assertion of p is motivated by S's belief that p.
conditions: asserts p
believes p
assertion motivated by belief that p
counterexample: A politician believes that taxes should be raised. He asserts that taxes should be raised. And his assertion is motivated by his belief — he is expressing what he genuinely thinks. All three conditions are met. Yet his assertion is made in a context where he knows his audience will interpret his words ironically — it is a well-known rhetorical convention in that chamber that proposals are advanced by their opponents in order to subject them to ridicule. His assertion, though sincere in the sense captured by the three conditions, functions communicatively as an insincere one. He knows this and intends it. The sincerity of the assertion, properly understood, depends on how it functions in the communicative context, not merely on the match between assertion, belief, and motivation.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved assertions made in straightforward communicative contexts where the connection between assertion, belief, and uptake was transparent. The definition, generalising from these, captured sincerity as a matter of inner states but neglected the communicative function of the assertion, which may diverge from the inner states when contextual conventions alter the pragmatic force of the speech act.
passage:
Consider the following cases of sincere assertion. A man says it is raining because he believes it is; his assertion is motivated by his belief. A witness testifies that she saw the defendant at the scene because she believes she did; her testimony is motivated by her belief. A teacher tells her pupils that the earth is approximately spherical because she believes this; her statement is motivated by her belief. In each case, there is an assertion, a corresponding belief, and the assertion is motivated by the belief. From these cases one might propose: S sincerely asserts p if and only if S asserts p, S believes p, and S's assertion is motivated by S's belief that p.

Yet this analysis, though adequate for the cases from which it was drawn, may be challenged. Consider a politician who believes that taxes ought to be raised. In a parliamentary debate, he asserts that taxes should be raised, motivated by his genuine conviction. The three conditions are satisfied. But the assertion is made in a chamber where a well-established rhetorical convention dictates that proposals are advanced by their opponents in order to expose them to ridicule. Every member of the audience understands the politician's assertion as ironic — as an indirect way of arguing against the tax increase. The politician knows this and intends his assertion to function ironically. His inner states — belief, motivation — are sincere, yet the communicative act is, by the standards of the context, insincere: it is designed to convey the opposite of what it literally says. The background cases all involved straightforward communicative contexts in which assertion, belief, and uptake were transparently aligned. The definition, generalising from these, located sincerity entirely in the relationship between assertion and inner states, and neglected the communicative dimension — how the assertion functions in its context. It thereby classifies as sincere an assertion which, in its operative communicative setting, is a vehicle of irony.

background_cases: A scientist who discovers that her experimental results are contaminated ceases to believe the hypothesis they supported. Her credence drops from high to low, and she revises accordingly.
A traveller who learns that the bridge ahead has collapsed ceases to believe it is safe to proceed. His credence drops sharply, and he turns back.
A voter who reads a convincing exposé of her candidate's corruption ceases to believe in his integrity. Her credence drops, and she changes her vote.
definition: S ceases to believe p if and only if (i) S previously believed p, (ii) S's credence in p drops below the threshold required for belief, and (iii) S no longer acts as though p were true.
conditions: previously believed p
credence drops below belief threshold
no longer acts as though p were true
counterexample: An elderly man who once believed firmly in the reliability of the postal service gradually loses confidence as letters go astray with increasing frequency. His credence in the reliability of the post drops well below any reasonable threshold for belief. Yet he continues to post letters, to expect replies, and to complain when they fail to arrive — acting, in all outward respects, as though the post were reliable. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not: he still acts as though p were true. Yet he does not believe p — he would tell you, with some vehemence, that the post is unreliable. His actions are products of habit, not belief. The definition, by making action a necessary condition of ceasing to believe, cannot accommodate cases in which habitual behaviour persists after belief has been abandoned.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved subjects whose actions changed in concert with their credences — when belief was lost, action followed. The definition, generalising from this pattern, treated the cessation of consonant action as a necessary sign of lost belief. But action may persist through habit even after the belief which originally motivated it has been abandoned, and the definition cannot distinguish habitual action from belief-driven action.
passage:
Consider the following cases in which belief is lost. A scientist whose experimental results prove contaminated ceases to believe the hypothesis they supported; her credence drops and she revises her views. A traveller who learns the bridge ahead has collapsed ceases to believe it safe to proceed; his credence drops and he turns back. A voter who reads a convincing exposé ceases to believe in her candidate's integrity; her credence drops and she changes her vote. In each case, the loss of belief is accompanied by a change in action. From these cases one might extract the definition: S ceases to believe p if and only if S previously believed p, S's credence in p drops below the threshold required for belief, and S no longer acts as though p were true.

This analysis, however, may be challenged. Consider an elderly man who once believed firmly in the reliability of the postal service. Over many years, as letters have gone astray with increasing frequency, his confidence has eroded. His credence in the post's reliability has dropped well below any plausible threshold for belief; he would tell you, with considerable feeling, that the post is thoroughly unreliable. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet he continues to post letters, to wait for replies, and to complain bitterly when they fail to arrive. He acts, in every observable respect, as though the postal service were reliable. The third condition is not met, and the definition accordingly refuses to say that he has ceased to believe. But he has ceased to believe — his verbal reports, his avowals, his self-understanding all attest to this. His actions are the products of long-standing habit, not of present belief. The background cases, from which the definition was drawn, all involved subjects whose actions changed cleanly in step with their credences. The definition, generalising from this tidy correspondence, treated the cessation of belief-consonant action as a necessary mark of belief's disappearance. It thereby failed to accommodate the stubborn persistence of habitual action in the absence of the belief which originally set the habit in motion.

background_cases: A man sees a red apple on the table: the apple is present, causes his experience, and his experience represents the apple accurately. This is a paradigm of veridical perception.
A woman hears a bell ringing: the bell is present, causes her auditory experience, and her experience represents the sound accurately.
A child touches a warm surface: the surface is present, causes his tactile experience, and his experience represents the warmth accurately.
definition: S has a veridical perception that p if and only if (i) there is an external state of affairs which makes p true, (ii) S's sensory experience represents p, and (iii) S's experience is caused by the state of affairs which makes p true.
conditions: external state of affairs makes p true
sensory experience represents p
experience caused by the truth-making state of affairs
counterexample: A man stands at the edge of a desert and sees what appears to be an oasis ahead. As it happens, there is indeed an oasis at that location — the state of affairs which makes p true obtains. His sensory experience represents the presence of the oasis. But his experience is caused not by the oasis itself but by the refraction of light from a different source — a mirage. The oasis is real, and the mirage happens to be spatially coincident with it. All three conditions are satisfied: there is a truth-making state of affairs, his experience represents it, and his experience is caused by something at the location of the truth-making state of affairs. Yet his perception is not veridical in the fullest sense, for the causal chain is deviant — his experience is caused by atmospheric refraction, not by the oasis itself.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved simple, unmediated causal chains running from the perceived object to the experience. The definition, generalising from these transparent cases, required only that the experience be caused by the truth-making state of affairs, but did not specify the kind of causal chain required. This omission leaves the definition vulnerable to deviant causal chains in which the experience is caused by the right state of affairs in the wrong way.
passage:
Consider the following paradigms of veridical perception. A man sees a red apple on the table: the apple causes his experience, and his experience represents it accurately. A woman hears a bell: the bell causes her auditory experience, and the representation is faithful. A child touches a warm surface: the surface causes his tactile experience, and the warmth is accurately represented. From these cases one might extract the definition: S has a veridical perception that p if and only if there is an external state of affairs making p true, S's sensory experience represents p, and the experience is caused by the truth-making state of affairs.

Yet this analysis admits cases which we should not count as veridical perception in the fullest sense. Suppose a man at the edge of a desert who sees what appears to be an oasis in the distance. There is, in fact, an oasis at precisely that location — the truth-making state of affairs obtains. His visual experience represents the presence of the oasis, and something at the location of the oasis is causally responsible for his experience. But the causal chain is deviant: what produces his experience is not the light reflected from the oasis itself but a mirage — an atmospheric refraction of light from a different source which happens to create an image coincident with the real oasis. The three conditions are, at least on a liberal reading, satisfied. Yet the perception is not veridical in the way the background cases are, for the subject's experience is produced by the wrong kind of causal process. The background cases involved simple, direct causal chains; the definition, generalising from these, required only that the truth-making state of affairs be the cause, without specifying what kind of causal connection is appropriate. It thereby admits cases in which the experience happens to match the world but is produced by a wayward causal route.

background_cases: When one looks at a straight stick half-submerged in water and it appears bent, this is an illusion: the stick is present, but one's experience misrepresents its shape.
When one sees railway lines converging in the distance, this is an illusion: the lines are parallel, but one's experience misrepresents their geometry.
When one hears a stationary sound source which seems to shift location as one turns one's head, this is an illusion: the source is fixed, but one's experience misrepresents its position.
definition: A perceptual experience is illusory if and only if (i) an external object is present and causally responsible for the experience, (ii) the experience misrepresents at least one property of the object, and (iii) the misrepresentation is due to the conditions of perception rather than to any defect in the perceiver.
conditions: external object present and causally responsible
experience misrepresents at least one property
misrepresentation due to conditions of perception not perceiver defect
counterexample: Consider a subject who takes a small dose of a substance which slightly alters his colour perception, making reds appear slightly more orange. He looks at a red post box and sees it as orange. The post box is present and causally responsible for his experience; the experience misrepresents its colour. But the misrepresentation is due to a temporary alteration in the perceiver — a pharmacological effect — rather than to the conditions of perception (lighting, medium, distance). The third condition is not met, and the definition classifies this as something other than an illusion. Yet the phenomenology is indistinguishable from a standard illusion of colour, and the subject would describe it in exactly those terms. The boundary between perceiver defect and perceptual condition is not as clear as the definition assumes.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved misrepresentation attributable to external conditions — water, distance, head movement — with no suggestion of perceiver defect. The definition, generalising from these, located the source of illusion exclusively in the conditions of perception, thereby drawing a sharp line between illusion and perceiver-based misperception. But this line proves porous: temporary pharmacological alterations produce phenomena phenomenologically identical to standard illusions, and the definition cannot accommodate them.
passage:
Consider the following cases of perceptual illusion. A straight stick half-submerged in water appears bent: the stick is present, but refraction through the water causes the experience to misrepresent its shape. Railway lines appear to converge in the distance: the lines are parallel, but perspective causes the misrepresentation. A stationary sound source seems to shift as one turns one's head: the source is fixed, but the movement of the head alters the apparent location. In each case, an external object is present and causally responsible, the experience misrepresents a property, and the misrepresentation is due to the conditions of perception — the medium, the distance, the relative motion. From these cases one might define: a perceptual experience is illusory if and only if an external object is present and causally responsible, the experience misrepresents at least one property, and the misrepresentation is due to the conditions of perception rather than to any defect in the perceiver.

This analysis, however, may be challenged. Suppose a subject who has taken a small dose of a substance which slightly alters his colour perception, causing reds to appear orange. He looks at a red post box and sees it as orange. The post box is present and causally responsible; the experience misrepresents its colour. But the source of the misrepresentation is not the conditions of perception — the lighting is normal, the distance is ordinary, there is no intervening medium — but a temporary alteration in the perceiver himself. The third condition fails, and the definition refuses to classify the experience as an illusion. Yet from the subject's point of view, the experience is phenomenologically indistinguishable from a standard colour illusion. The background cases all involved misrepresentation traceable to external conditions, and the definition, generalising from these, located the source of illusion outside the perceiver. It thereby drew a sharp boundary between illusion and perceiver-based misperception which the phenomenology does not support, for temporary alterations of the perceiver may produce experiences indistinguishable in character from paradigmatic illusions.

background_cases: A man sees a tree and knows, by looking, that there is a tree before him. His knowledge is perceptual and direct — he does not infer the tree's presence from anything else.
A woman tastes salt in her soup and knows, by tasting, that the soup is salty. Her knowledge is immediate and perceptual.
A child hears thunder and knows, by hearing, that thunder is occurring. His knowledge is gained directly through perception.
definition: S has direct perceptual knowledge that p if and only if (i) S perceives that p, (ii) S's perceptual experience is the sole basis for S's belief that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is true.
conditions: perceives that p
perceptual experience is sole basis for belief
belief is true
counterexample: A radiologist looks at an X-ray and perceives that the patient's femur is fractured. She perceives this — she sees the fracture line on the film. Her perceptual experience is the sole basis for her belief. And the belief is true — the femur is indeed fractured. All three conditions are met. Yet her perception is thoroughly dependent upon years of specialised training; an untrained person looking at the same X-ray would perceive nothing of diagnostic significance. Her perceptual knowledge, whilst in one sense direct — she sees the fracture — is in another sense deeply mediated by learned interpretive frameworks. The definition, by requiring only that perception be the sole basis, does not distinguish between the unmediated perception of the background cases and the expertise-dependent perception of the radiologist.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved perceptual knowledge available to any normally constituted perceiver — seeing a tree, tasting salt, hearing thunder. The definition, generalising from these cases of universally accessible perception, required only that perception be the sole basis and the belief be true. It failed to note that the background perceptions did not depend on specialised training, and so it cannot distinguish perception which is direct in the strong sense — accessible to all — from perception which, though experientially immediate, is cognitively mediated by expertise.
passage:

A woman tastes salt in her soup and knows, by tasting, that the soup is salty. A child hears thunder and knows, by hearing, that thunder is occurring. In each case, the subject perceives that p, the perceptual experience is the sole basis for the belief, and the belief is true. From these cases one might extract: S has direct perceptual knowledge that p if and only if S perceives that p, S's perceptual experience is the sole basis for the belief, and the belief is true.

This analysis, however, proves inadequate when applied to certain cases. Consider a radiologist who examines an X-ray film and perceives that the patient's femur is fractured. She sees the fracture line; her perceptual experience is the sole basis for her belief; and the belief is true. The three conditions are met, and the definition classifies her knowledge as direct perceptual knowledge. Yet there is something misleading about this classification. An untrained person looking at the same film would see only a pattern of light and dark with no diagnostic significance. The radiologist's perception, though experientially immediate — she does not consciously infer the fracture from premisses — is deeply dependent upon years of specialised training which have shaped her perceptual system to detect features invisible to the novice. The background cases all involved perceptual knowledge available to any normally constituted perceiver: anyone with functioning eyes can see a tree, anyone with a normal palate can taste salt. The definition, generalising from these universally accessible cases, required only that perception be the sole basis and the belief true, and took no account of the degree to which the perceptual capacity itself may be a product of learned expertise. It thereby classifies as direct a form of perceptual knowledge which is, in an important sense, cognitively mediated.

background_cases: When I see a red surface, I am aware of redness; the redness is a property of my experience, and it is also a property of the surface. My experience matches the world.
When I hear a loud sound, I am aware of loudness; the loudness characterises both my experience and the sound. My experience matches the world.
When I feel a rough texture, I am aware of roughness; the roughness characterises both my experience and the surface I touch. My experience matches the world.
definition: A secondary quality Q is a real property of an external object O if and only if (i) O produces in normal perceivers under standard conditions an experience of Q, (ii) the experience of Q is systematically correlated with certain physical features of O, and (iii) different objects produce different experiences of Q in virtue of their different physical features.
conditions: O produces experience of Q in normal perceivers under standard conditions
experience systematically correlated with physical features
different objects produce different Q-experiences via different features
counterexample: Consider metameric colours — pairs of surfaces with different spectral reflectance profiles which nonetheless produce exactly the same colour experience in normal perceivers under standard conditions. Surface A produces the experience of green, and so does surface B; the experience is the same, yet the physical features are different. Condition (iii) states that different objects produce different experiences in virtue of different physical features, but here we have different features producing the same experience. The correlation between experience and physical feature is many-to-one, not one-to-one. The definition assumes a tighter correlation than actually obtains.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved simple correspondences between experienced qualities and physical features: one surface, one colour experience, one physical basis. The definition, generalising from these one-to-one cases, assumed that different physical features would produce different experiences. But the phenomenon of metamerism — in which physically distinct surfaces are perceptually indistinguishable — shows that the mapping from physical features to experienced qualities is many-to-one, and the definition cannot accommodate this.
passage:
Consider the following cases in which secondary qualities appear to be real properties of external objects. When one sees a red surface, redness characterises both the experience and the surface. When one hears a loud sound, loudness characterises both the experience and the sound. When one feels a rough texture, roughness characterises both the experience and the surface. In each case, there is a satisfying correspondence between the experienced quality and some feature of the external object. From such cases one might extract: a secondary quality Q is a real property of an external object O if and only if O produces in normal perceivers under standard conditions an experience of Q, the experience is systematically correlated with certain physical features of O, and different objects produce different experiences of Q in virtue of their different physical features.

This analysis, however, encounters a difficulty when confronted with the phenomenon of metamerism. Two surfaces may have entirely different spectral reflectance profiles — different physical features — and yet produce exactly the same colour experience in normal perceivers under standard conditions. Surface A reflects light at certain wavelengths, and surface B reflects light at quite different wavelengths, yet both appear identically green. The third condition states that different objects produce different experiences in virtue of their different physical features. But here we have objects with different physical features producing the same experience. The mapping from physical feature to experienced quality is many-to-one, not one-to-one as the definition assumes. The background cases involved simple, transparent correspondences between qualities and features, and the definition, generalising from these tidy cases, presupposed a tighter correlation than the facts admit. The phenomenon of metamerism reveals that secondary qualities, if they are real properties, are not as closely tied to specific physical features as the definition requires.

background_cases: A person who hallucinates a pink elephant in his drawing room has an experience which is entirely subjective: there is no pink elephant, and his experience does not put him in contact with the world.
A woman who hallucinates voices has an auditory experience with no external source: the experience is entirely generated within her own mind.
A fevered child who hallucinates a monster at the foot of his bed has a visual experience with no corresponding external object.
definition: A hallucination H and a corresponding veridical perception V are of fundamentally different kinds if and only if (i) V is constituted in part by the perceived object whilst H is not constituted by any external object, (ii) V puts the subject in direct contact with a mind-independent particular whilst H does not, and (iii) there is no phenomenal property which is common to both V and H.
conditions: V constituted by perceived object, H not constituted by external object
V provides direct contact with mind-independent particular, H does not
no phenomenal property common to V and H
counterexample: Consider a subject who has a veridical perception of a lemon — yellow, ovoid, glossy — and on a separate occasion a perfect hallucination of an identical lemon. The veridical perception is constituted by the lemon; the hallucination is not constituted by any external object; the veridical perception provides contact with a mind-independent particular whilst the hallucination does not. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met. But condition (iii) — that there is no common phenomenal property — seems impossible to sustain. Both experiences have the phenomenal property of yellowness, of ovoid shape, of glossy surface. To deny any commonality is to deny what seems phenomenologically undeniable. The disjunctivist may insist that the phenomenal properties are only apparently the same, but this response threatens to make the theory unfalsifiable.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved hallucinations which were manifestly different from any veridical perception — pink elephants, disembodied voices, monsters — making it easy to maintain that hallucination and veridical perception share no phenomenal common ground. The definition, generalising from these dramatic cases, denied any phenomenal overlap. But when the hallucination exactly duplicates a veridical perception, the denial of common phenomenal properties becomes phenomenologically implausible.
passage:
Consider the following cases of hallucination. A man hallucinates a pink elephant in his drawing room: there is no such creature, and his experience is entirely subjective. A woman hallucinates voices with no external source. A fevered child hallucinates a monster at the foot of his bed. In each case, the hallucination is plainly different in kind from veridical perception: there is no object, no contact with the world, and the phenomenal character of the experience seems peculiar to the hallucinatory state. From such cases one might extract the disjunctivist thesis: a hallucination and a corresponding veridical perception are of fundamentally different kinds if and only if the veridical perception is constituted by the perceived object whilst the hallucination is not, the veridical perception provides direct contact with a mind-independent particular whilst the hallucination does not, and there is no phenomenal property common to both.

Yet this analysis encounters a serious difficulty when the hallucination precisely duplicates a veridical perception. Suppose a subject who on one occasion has a veridical perception of a lemon — yellow, ovoid, glossy — and on another occasion has a hallucination phenomenologically indistinguishable from this perception. The first two conditions are met: the veridical perception is constituted by the lemon, the hallucination is not. But the third condition — the denial of any common phenomenal property — seems phenomenologically untenable. Both experiences present yellowness, ovoid shape, and glossy surface. To insist that these phenomenal properties are merely apparently similar, not genuinely shared, is to adopt a position which no observation could refute. The background cases involved hallucinations markedly different from ordinary perception — pink elephants, disembodied voices — and the denial of phenomenal overlap was correspondingly easy to maintain. The definition, by generalising from such dramatic cases, denied commonality across the board, and thereby committed itself to a claim which becomes increasingly implausible as the hallucination approaches perfect fidelity to veridical experience.

background_cases: When I see that the kettle is boiling, I gain knowledge that the kettle is boiling. My visual perception justifies my belief without the need for inference.
When I hear my neighbour's dog barking, I gain knowledge that the dog is barking. My auditory perception provides immediate justification.
When I smell smoke, I gain knowledge that something is burning nearby. My olfactory perception provides immediate justification.
definition: Perception provides immediate justification for a belief that p if and only if (i) S has a perceptual experience with the content that p, (ii) S forms the belief that p on the basis of this experience, and (iii) the justification does not depend on S's having any other justified beliefs.
conditions: perceptual experience with content p
belief formed on basis of experience
justification does not depend on other justified beliefs
counterexample: A monolingual English speaker enters a room where a sign in Chinese characters reads (unknown to her) 'Danger: radiation.' She has a perceptual experience — she sees the characters clearly. But her experience does not have the content that there is radiation; it has the content that there are certain shapes on the wall. She cannot form the belief that there is danger on the basis of her perception, because her perceptual experience lacks the relevant content. Condition (i) fails, and rightly so. But now consider a Chinese reader who sees the same sign. His experience has the content that there is danger. Has the perceptual experience itself changed, or has background knowledge — knowledge of Chinese — made a different content available? If the latter, then the justification depends upon prior justified beliefs (about the meaning of Chinese characters), and condition (iii) fails. The definition cannot accommodate the role of background knowledge in determining perceptual content.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved perceptual contents — boiling kettles, barking dogs, smoke — accessible to any normally constituted perceiver without specialised knowledge. The definition, generalising from these, held that perceptual justification is independent of other justified beliefs. But the content of a perceptual experience may itself be partly determined by the subject's background knowledge, and in such cases the justification is not as independent as the definition requires.
passage:
Consider the following cases of perceptual justification. When one sees that the kettle is boiling, one gains justified belief that the kettle is boiling, and this justification seems independent of any other beliefs one holds. When one hears the dog barking, one gains justified belief without inference from premisses. When one smells smoke, one gains justified belief that something is burning. In each case, the perceptual experience provides immediate, non-inferential justification. From these cases one might extract: perception provides immediate justification for a belief that p if and only if S has a perceptual experience with the content p, forms the belief on this basis, and the justification does not depend on S's having other justified beliefs.

This analysis, however, encounters a difficulty when we consider cases in which perceptual content is shaped by background knowledge. Suppose a monolingual English speaker who enters a room displaying a sign in Chinese characters meaning 'Danger: radiation.' She sees the characters clearly but cannot form the belief that there is danger, for her experience lacks the relevant content. Now suppose a Chinese reader who sees the same sign. His perceptual experience apparently has the content that there is danger — he sees the warning. But this content is available to him only because he possesses background knowledge of Chinese. If the content of his experience is partly determined by his prior justified beliefs about the meaning of the characters, then the justification his experience provides is not independent of other justified beliefs; condition (iii) fails. The background cases all involved perceptual contents universally accessible to normally constituted perceivers — anyone can see a boiling kettle, hear a bark, smell smoke. The definition, generalising from these epistemically simple cases, held that perceptual justification requires no support from other beliefs. But it did not anticipate cases in which the very content of the experience depends upon knowledge the subject has acquired through prior learning.

background_cases: A man sees a red post box and reports that it is red. His visual experience has a definite phenomenal character — redness — and this character corresponds to a feature of the object.
A woman sees a green lawn and reports that it is green. Her experience has a definite phenomenal character which corresponds to the lawn's colour.
A child sees a yellow daffodil and reports that it is yellow. His experience has a definite phenomenal character corresponding to the flower's colour.
definition: The phenomenal character of a colour experience is determined by the external object if and only if (i) the experience has a definite colour quality, (ii) the colour quality corresponds to a physical property of the object, and (iii) variation in the object's physical properties produces corresponding variation in the experienced colour quality.
conditions: experience has definite colour quality
quality corresponds to physical property of object
variation in physical properties produces corresponding variation in quality
counterexample: Consider the well-documented phenomenon of colour constancy: a white sheet of paper viewed under reddish tungsten light appears white, even though the light it reflects to the eye is predominantly in the red portion of the spectrum. The experience has a definite colour quality (white), but this quality does not correspond straightforwardly to the physical property of the reflected light (which is reddish). Furthermore, the variation condition fails in a striking way: changing the illumination from daylight to tungsten dramatically changes the physical properties of the reflected light but does not change the experienced colour. The experienced quality remains constant whilst the physical properties vary. The definition, which assumes a tight tracking relation, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of colour constancy.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved objects viewed under normal daylight conditions where the correspondence between physical reflectance and experienced colour was straightforward. The definition, generalising from these optimal conditions, assumed a direct tracking relation between physical properties and experienced qualities. Colour constancy reveals that the visual system actively compensates for changes in illumination, so that experienced colour remains stable even when the physical stimulus changes dramatically — a phenomenon the definition cannot capture.
passage:
Consider the following cases of colour perception. A man sees a red post box and reports redness; his experience has a definite colour quality corresponding to the object's reflectance. A woman sees a green lawn and reports greenness; again, the quality corresponds to a physical property. A child sees a yellow daffodil and reports yellowness. In each case, the experience has a definite colour quality, this quality corresponds to a property of the object, and changes in the object's properties would produce corresponding changes in the experienced quality. From these cases one might propose: the phenomenal character of a colour experience is determined by the external object if and only if the experience has a definite colour quality, this quality corresponds to a physical property of the object, and variation in the physical properties produces corresponding variation in the experienced quality.

Yet this analysis encounters a well-known difficulty. Consider the phenomenon of colour constancy. A white sheet of paper viewed under the reddish light of a tungsten bulb continues to appear white, despite the fact that the light reflected from it to the eye is predominantly red. The experience has a definite colour quality — whiteness — but this quality does not correspond to the physical character of the reflected light, which is reddish. More strikingly still, the physical properties of the stimulus vary dramatically as the illumination changes from daylight to tungsten, yet the experienced colour quality remains constant. The third condition — that variation in physical properties produces corresponding variation in experienced quality — is plainly violated. The background cases all involved objects viewed under conditions in which the correspondence between physical reflectance and experienced colour was transparent and direct. The definition, generalising from these favourable cases, assumed that the visual system passively tracks the physical stimulus. Colour constancy reveals that the system actively compensates for changes in illumination, maintaining perceptual stability at the cost of the very correspondence upon which the definition depends.

background_cases: When I reach out and grasp a cup, my visual perception of the cup's location guides my hand accurately to the target. Perception and action are seamlessly coordinated.
When I step over an obstacle on the pavement, my visual perception of the obstacle's height and position guides my leg accurately. Perception serves action.
When I catch a ball thrown to me, my visual perception of the ball's trajectory guides my hands to the right place at the right time.
definition: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features of the environment, (ii) the content of this experience determines S's motor response, and (iii) the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the perceptual experience.
conditions: conscious perceptual experience of relevant features
content determines motor response
accuracy of action depends on accuracy of experience
counterexample: Consider a patient with visual-form agnosia (damage to the ventral visual stream) who cannot consciously recognise objects or report their orientation, yet who can accurately reach out and grasp them with the appropriate hand aperture. She has no conscious perceptual experience of the object's shape — she cannot describe it or draw it. Yet her motor response is accurately determined by visual information, and the accuracy of her action depends on the accuracy of this visual processing. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not: there is no conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features. Yet perception — in the form of unconscious visuomotor processing — guides her action with remarkable precision.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved normally sighted subjects in whom conscious perception and motor guidance were seamlessly unified. The definition, generalising from these integrated cases, made conscious perceptual experience a necessary condition for perception's guidance of action. But the neuropsychological evidence from visual-form agnosia shows that accurate visuomotor guidance can proceed in the complete absence of conscious perceptual experience, revealing a dissociation which the definition cannot accommodate.
passage:
Consider the following cases of perception guiding action. When one reaches out to grasp a cup, visual perception of the cup's location guides the hand accurately. When one steps over a kerb, visual perception of its height guides the leg. When one catches a ball, perception of its trajectory guides the hands to the right position. In each case, the subject has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant environmental features, the content of this experience determines the motor response, and the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the experience. From these cases one might propose: perception guides action if and only if the subject has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features, the content of this experience determines the motor response, and the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the experience.

This analysis, however, is challenged by a striking neuropsychological dissociation. Consider a patient with visual-form agnosia — damage to the ventral stream of visual processing — who cannot consciously recognise the shapes and orientations of objects placed before her. She cannot report whether a slot is oriented vertically or horizontally; she cannot describe or draw the objects she sees. Yet when asked to post a card through the slot, she orients it correctly with ease. When asked to grasp an object, her hand aperture adjusts accurately to the object's size and shape. Her motor responses are guided by visual information with remarkable precision, but she has no conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features. Condition (i) fails entirely. The background cases involved normally sighted subjects in whom conscious experience and motor guidance were seamlessly unified, and the definition, generalising from this unity, made conscious experience a prerequisite. The neuropsychological evidence reveals that the unity is contingent rather than necessary: perception can guide action through channels which bypass conscious experience altogether.

background_cases: A man walks into a room and immediately perceives that there is a table in it. He does not infer the table's existence from patches of colour and shape; he simply sees the table.
A woman enters a garden and perceives a rose. She does not construct the rose from sensory atoms; the rose is given to her whole in perception.
A child looks up and perceives the moon. He does not infer its presence from luminous data; he sees the moon directly.
definition: S directly perceives a physical object O if and only if (i) S's perceptual experience presents O as a unified, three-dimensional object, (ii) S's awareness of O is not mediated by awareness of any subjective sensory state, and (iii) O is presented as existing independently of S's perceiving it.
conditions: experience presents O as unified three-dimensional object
awareness not mediated by subjective sensory state
O presented as existing independently of perceiving
counterexample: Consider a subject who, having donned a virtual-reality headset, finds herself in a computer-generated environment. She sees what appears to be a table before her. Her experience presents the table as a unified, three-dimensional object; she is not aware of any subjective sensory state mediating her perception; and the table is presented as existing independently of her perceiving it. All three conditions are met. Yet she does not directly perceive a physical object, for there is no physical table — only a digital simulation. The definition, by attending to the phenomenological character of the experience without requiring that a physical object actually be present and causally responsible, admits virtual objects as objects of direct perception.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved real physical objects — tables, roses, the moon — present in the subject's environment and causally responsible for the experience. The definition, generalising from these, captured the phenomenological features of direct perception — unity, immediacy, independence — but neglected to include the requirement that the object be a real physical object causally producing the experience, because in the background cases this went without saying.
passage:
Consider the following cases of direct perception. A man walks into a room and sees a table: the table is given to him as a unified, three-dimensional object, without any mediating awareness of sensory states, and it is presented as existing independently of his perceiving it. A woman enters a garden and perceives a rose in the same direct, unmediated fashion. A child looks up and perceives the moon. From such cases one might extract: S directly perceives a physical object O if and only if S's experience presents O as a unified three-dimensional object, S's awareness is not mediated by any subjective sensory state, and O is presented as existing independently of S's perceiving it.

This analysis captures the phenomenology of direct perception admirably, yet it may be shown to be insufficient by the following case. Suppose a subject who dons a sophisticated virtual-reality headset and finds herself in a computer-generated room. Before her stands what appears to be a solid oak table. Her experience presents the table as a unified, three-dimensional object, complete with grain and shadow. She is not aware of any sensory intermediary — the headset is forgotten, and the experience is phenomenologically immediate. The table is presented as existing independently of her perceiving it; she experiences it as part of a world that would persist in her absence. All three conditions are met. Yet she does not directly perceive a physical object, for there is no physical table — only a pattern of pixels. The background cases all involved real objects causally present in the subject's environment, and this fact was so obvious as to go unmentioned. The definition, generalising from the phenomenological features of these cases, captured what direct perception is like from the inside but omitted the external, causal condition which ensures that the phenomenology is anchored to reality. Without this condition, virtual objects satisfy the definition as readily as physical ones.

background_cases: A traveller asks a local resident for directions to the railway station; the resident, having lived in the town for thirty years, sincerely and correctly indicates the route, and the traveller thereby comes to know the way.

A student asks her chemistry lecturer whether copper sulphate is soluble in water; the lecturer, drawing upon genuine expertise, affirms that it is, and the student acquires this knowledge on the basis of the lecturer's word.

A juror listens to an eyewitness who was present at the scene of an incident, who sincerely and accurately recounts what she observed, and the juror comes to know certain facts about the event through this testimony.
definition: A hearer H comes to know that p through testimony if and only if: (1) a speaker S asserts that p to H; (2) S sincerely believes that p; (3) S is competent with respect to the subject-matter of p; and (4) p is true.
conditions: S asserts that p to H
S sincerely believes that p
S is competent with respect to the subject-matter of p
p is true
counterexample: A seasoned meteorologist, possessed of genuine competence and unimpeachable sincerity, consults her instruments and tells a colleague that a severe storm will strike the region within twelve hours. Unbeknownst to her, the instruments have been subtly miscalibrated by an electrical fault, and the storm does in fact arrive — but only because of an entirely unrelated weather system that her instruments did not detect. She sincerely believed her assertion, she was competent, and the proposition turned out true, yet it seems plain that the colleague does not come to know that a storm is approaching through her testimony, for the connection between her grounds and the truth of the matter is wholly accidental.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involved testifiers whose competence was causally responsible for the truth of what they asserted reaching the hearer in the right way. The definition, however, failed to capture this requirement that the testifier's competence must be appropriately connected to the truth of the proposition — a species of the broader Gettier problem as it arises within the testimonial domain.

We may begin by considering several familiar instances in which one person comes to know something on the strength of another's word. A traveller, finding himself in an unfamiliar town, asks a local resident for directions to the railway station. The resident, having lived in the town for the better part of thirty years, sincerely and correctly indicates the route; and we should say, without hesitation, that the traveller thereby comes to know the way. Similarly, a student enquires of her chemistry lecturer whether copper sulphate is soluble in water. The lecturer, drawing upon genuine expertise, affirms that it is, and we should again be inclined to say that the student acquires knowledge on the basis of this testimony. A third case presents itself in the context of legal proceedings: a juror listens to an eyewitness who was present at the scene of a disputed incident, and who sincerely and accurately recounts what she observed. Here too, we should ordinarily grant that the juror comes to know certain facts about the event through testimony.
