From these paradigm cases one might extract the following analysis: a hearer H comes to know that p through testimony if and only if a speaker S asserts that p to H, S sincerely believes that p, S is competent with respect to the subject-matter of p, and p is true. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a seasoned meteorologist, possessed of genuine competence and unimpeachable sincerity, consults her instruments and tells a colleague that a severe storm will strike the region within twelve hours. Unbeknownst to her, the instruments have been subtly miscalibrated by an electrical fault. The storm does in fact arrive, but only because of an entirely unrelated weather system that her instruments failed to detect. All four conditions of our analysis are satisfied — she asserted the proposition, she believed it sincerely, she is competent in meteorology, and the proposition is true — yet it would be exceedingly odd to say that the colleague knows, on the basis of her testimony, that the storm is approaching. The truth of her assertion and her grounds for making it are connected only by coincidence. What the background cases shared, and what the definition neglected to require, is that the testifier's competence must stand in an appropriate causal or explanatory relation to the truth of the proposition communicated. This is, in effect, a testimonial analogue of the well-known Gettier problem: mere satisfaction of belief, truth, competence, and sincerity does not suffice for knowledge when the route from competence to truth passes through accident rather than through the exercise of the very capacity that renders the speaker authoritative.

background_cases: A historian reads an account of a medieval battle in a chronicle and, having independently confirmed the chronicle's claims against archaeological evidence and other documents, accepts the account as knowledge.

A detective hears an informant's claim that a suspect was seen at a particular location and, having corroborated this through CCTV footage and telephone records, comes to know the suspect's whereabouts.

A physician is told by a patient that she has been experiencing chest pains; the physician, having confirmed this through an electrocardiogram and blood tests, accepts the patient's report as accurate.
definition: A hearer H is justified in accepting testimony that p if and only if: (1) a speaker S testifies that p to H; (2) H possesses independent evidence that bears positively on the truth of p; and (3) the conjunction of S's testimony and H's independent evidence makes p more probable than not.
conditions: A speaker S testifies that p to H
H possesses independent evidence that bears positively on the truth of p
The conjunction of S's testimony and H's independent evidence makes p more probable than not
counterexample: A young child is told by her mother that there are nine planets in the solar system. The child possesses no independent evidence whatever bearing on the number of planets, yet it seems entirely proper — indeed, it would be perverse to deny — that the child is justified in believing what her mother tells her. The reductionist conditions are met by none of the child's epistemic resources, and yet the acceptance is paradigmatically rational.
abductive_insight: The background cases were drawn from contexts — historical research, criminal investigation, medical diagnosis — in which independent corroboration is both available and expected as a matter of professional practice. The definition mistakenly elevated this contextual feature into a universal requirement, overlooking the vast range of testimony we rightly accept without any independent check, particularly in early learning and ordinary life.
passage:
It has sometimes been maintained, in the tradition we may call reductionist, that testimonial justification must ultimately rest upon the hearer's own non-testimonial evidence. Consider the following cases which lend this view a measure of initial plausibility. A historian reads an account of a medieval battle in a monastic chronicle and, having independently confirmed the chronicle's claims against archaeological remains and corroborating documents, accepts the account as well-founded. A detective hears from an informant that a suspect was seen at a particular location and, having corroborated this claim through closed-circuit television footage and telephone records, arrives at knowledge of the suspect's movements. A physician is told by a patient that she has been experiencing chest pains; the physician, having confirmed the report through electrocardiographic readings and blood tests, accepts the patient's testimony as accurate. In each of these instances, we might observe, the hearer does not merely take the speaker's word for it, but supplements testimony with independent evidence. One might therefore propose the following reductionist analysis: a hearer is justified in accepting testimony that p if and only if a speaker testifies that p, the hearer possesses independent evidence bearing positively on the truth of p, and the conjunction of testimony and independent evidence renders p more probable than not.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. A young child is told by her mother that there are nine planets in the solar system. The child has no independent astronomical evidence whatever — she has never consulted a textbook, peered through a telescope, or so much as counted the planets on a chart. Nonetheless, it seems entirely proper to say that the child is justified in believing what her mother tells her. Indeed, it would be something approaching philosophical perversity to insist that she is not justified until she has independently corroborated the claim. The reductionist conditions are satisfied in none of the relevant respects, and yet the child's acceptance is paradigmatically rational. What the background cases shared was a feature of their particular epistemic contexts: historical enquiry, criminal investigation, and medical diagnosis are all domains in which independent corroboration is readily available and is expected as a matter of professional diligence. The analysis mistakenly promoted this contextual feature into a universal necessary condition, thereby excluding the enormous and indispensable role that uncorroborated testimony plays in the acquisition of knowledge, particularly during the formative years of a person's intellectual development and in the ordinary conduct of daily life.

background_cases: A visitor to a foreign city asks a passer-by for the time; the passer-by glances at her watch and replies, and the visitor, having no reason to doubt her, rightly accepts the answer.
A reader picks up a reputable encyclopaedia and, without further enquiry, accepts its claim regarding the population of Brazil, and is right to do so.
A patient is told by his dentist that he has a cavity in his upper left molar; having no reason to distrust the dentist, he accepts this on the dentist's authority and is correct in doing so.
definition: A hearer H is prima facie justified in accepting testimony that p if and only if: (1) a speaker S asserts that p to H; (2) H has no undefeated reason to doubt S's reliability; and (3) H has not been given positive reason to suspect that S is insincere or incompetent with respect to p.
conditions: A speaker S asserts that p to H
H has no undefeated reason to doubt S's reliability
H has not been given positive reason to suspect that S is insincere or incompetent with respect to p
counterexample: In a society where prevailing cultural assumptions systematically distort judgement on a particular matter — say, a community in which it is universally and sincerely believed, owing to longstanding tradition, that a certain local river is fed by underground springs when in fact it is fed by glacial melt — a newcomer who asks any resident about the river's source will receive a confident and sincere answer that is nonetheless false. The newcomer has no reason to doubt any particular speaker, no indication of insincerity, and no evidence of incompetence, yet default trust leads her into error because the entire community shares a systematic bias that poisons the well of testimony at its source.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved speakers whose reliability was, as it happened, underwritten by genuinely truth-conducive processes — a functioning watch, competent editorial oversight, professional dental training. The anti-reductionist definition captured the hearer's lack of reasons to doubt, but failed to require that the speaker's testimony be produced by processes that are in fact reliably connected to the truth, leaving the hearer vulnerable to communities of sincere but systematically mistaken testifiers.
passage:
The anti-reductionist about testimony holds that we are, in the absence of countervailing considerations, entitled to take others at their word. This view draws support from a range of everyday cases. A visitor to a foreign city asks a passer-by for the time; the passer-by glances at her watch, replies that it is half past two, and the visitor, having no reason to doubt her, accepts the answer — quite rightly, as it turns out. A reader picks up a reputable encyclopaedia and, without conducting any independent investigation, accepts its claim regarding the population of Brazil; again, we should not hesitate to say that she is justified in doing so. A patient is told by his dentist that he has a cavity in his upper left molar; having no particular reason to distrust the dentist, he accepts this on the dentist's authority, and is correct in doing so. From such cases one might extract an anti-reductionist principle: a hearer is prima facie justified in accepting testimony that p provided that a speaker asserts that p, the hearer has no undefeated reason to doubt the speaker's reliability, and the hearer has not been given positive reason to suspect insincerity or incompetence.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Imagine a community in which it is universally and sincerely believed, owing to a longstanding local tradition, that a certain river is fed by underground springs, when in truth it is fed by glacial melt from a distant mountain range. The belief has persisted for generations; no resident has ever had occasion to question it, and every resident would testify to it with perfect confidence and sincerity. A newcomer arrives and, quite naturally, asks a resident about the river's source. She receives a confident, unhesitating, and entirely sincere answer — and she has no reason whatever to doubt it. All three conditions of the anti-reductionist analysis are satisfied: the speaker asserts the proposition, the hearer has no undefeated reason to doubt the speaker, and there is no indication of insincerity or incompetence. Yet the newcomer is led into false belief, and it seems doubtful that we should call her acceptance justified in the fullest sense. What the background cases shared, and what the definition omitted, is that the speakers' reliability was underwritten by genuinely truth-conducive processes — a functioning timepiece, competent editorial oversight, professional dental training. Default trust, absent any requirement that the testimonial source be in fact reliably connected to the truth, leaves the hearer exposed to communities of sincere but systematically mistaken testifiers, where the very unanimity of opinion masks the absence of any adequate epistemic basis.

background_cases: A judge, seeking to understand the structural integrity of a building that has collapsed, consults a licensed civil engineer with decades of relevant experience, and rightly defers to her assessment.

A government minister, formulating public health policy, consults an epidemiologist who has published extensively in peer-reviewed journals on the disease in question, and is justified in relying upon her expert opinion.
A solicitor, advising a client on the tax implications of a transaction, consults a chartered accountant who specialises in corporate taxation, and properly relies on his professional judgement.
definition: A hearer H is justified in deferring to S's testimony that p on the grounds of S's expertise if and only if: (1) S possesses recognised credentials in a field F; (2) S has a substantial track record of reliable judgement in F; (3) p falls within the scope of enquiry conventionally associated with F; and (4) S asserts that p in S's capacity as an expert in F.
conditions: S possesses recognised credentials in a field F
S has a substantial track record of reliable judgement in F
p falls within the scope of enquiry conventionally associated with F
S asserts that p in S's capacity as an expert in F
counterexample: A Nobel laureate in physics, possessing the most distinguished credentials imaginable and an impeccable track record of reliable judgement within her own discipline, is invited to speak at a conference on nutrition science. She delivers a confident pronouncement on the optimal human diet, prefacing her remarks with reference to her scientific credentials and her general capacity for rigorous analysis. The proposition she asserts falls, let us suppose, within the scope of enquiry conventionally associated with nutritional science — a field in which she holds no qualification and has conducted no research. All four conditions of the analysis are formally satisfied if we allow that her credentials and track record in physics count as credentials and a track record in 'a field,' yet it would be plainly unreasonable to defer to her testimony on dietary matters on the strength of her expertise in quantum mechanics.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involved experts whose credentials and track records were domain-specific — the engineer opined on engineering, the epidemiologist on epidemiology, the accountant on taxation. The definition required credentials and reliability 'in a field' and that the proposition fall within 'the scope of enquiry associated with a field,' but it failed to require that these be the same field, thereby permitting the authority earned in one domain to be illicitly transferred to another.
passage:
When we defer to expert testimony, we do so on the assumption that the expert's training, experience, and demonstrated competence lend her assertions a special authority. Consider three cases which illustrate this thought. A judge, seeking to understand why a building has collapsed, consults a licensed civil engineer with decades of experience in structural analysis; the judge is plainly justified in deferring to her assessment. A government minister, formulating public health policy in the face of an emerging disease, consults an epidemiologist who has published extensively in the relevant peer-reviewed literature; the minister is right to rely upon her expert opinion. A solicitor, advising a client on the tax consequences of a proposed transaction, consults a chartered accountant who specialises in corporate taxation; the solicitor properly depends upon his professional judgement. From these cases one might distil the following conditions for justified deference to expert testimony: the speaker possesses recognised credentials in a field, has a substantial track record of reliable judgement in that field, the proposition asserted falls within the scope of enquiry conventionally associated with the field, and the speaker asserts the proposition in her capacity as an expert.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a Nobel laureate in theoretical physics — a scientist of the highest distinction, whose credentials are beyond reproach and whose track record of reliable judgement within her own discipline is unimpeachable — is invited to deliver an address at a conference on nutrition science. She offers a confident pronouncement regarding the optimal human diet, prefacing her remarks with reference to her scientific credentials and her capacity for rigorous analysis. The proposition she asserts falls, let us stipulate, within the scope of enquiry conventionally associated with nutritional science. If one reads the conditions of our analysis with insufficient care, they appear to be satisfied: she possesses recognised credentials in a field, she has a track record of reliability in a field, the proposition concerns a field, and she speaks in her capacity as an expert. Yet it would be manifestly unreasonable to defer to her authority on dietary matters simply because she is an eminent physicist. What the background cases had in common, and what the definition failed to make explicit, is that the expert's credentials, track record, and the proposition in question must all pertain to one and the same domain of competence. The analysis permitted the authority earned in one field to migrate, as it were, to another — a fallacy of misplaced expertise that the background cases, by happy coincidence, never exhibited.

background_cases: A woman tells her husband that she saw a fox in the garden this morning; the husband tells his colleague at work; the colleague mentions it to his wife over dinner. At each stage the speaker is sincere and competent, and the information is transmitted accurately.

A scientist publishes a finding in a journal; a textbook author summarises the finding; a lecturer conveys the summary to her students. Each link in the chain faithfully preserves the content of the original report.

A diplomat relays a foreign leader's stated position to his own head of state, who in turn communicates it to a parliamentary committee. The message passes through two intermediaries without distortion.
definition: A hearer H at the end of a testimonial chain is justified in believing that p if and only if: (1) the original speaker S₁ was justified in asserting that p; (2) each subsequent speaker Sₙ sincerely and accurately relays what Sₙ₋₁ asserted; and (3) no link in the chain introduces distortion or falsehood.
conditions: The original speaker S₁ was justified in asserting that p
Each subsequent speaker Sₙ sincerely and accurately relays what Sₙ₋₁ asserted
No link in the chain introduces distortion or falsehood
counterexample: An ornithologist observes a rare bird and records a precise description of its plumage, call, and habitat. She tells a colleague, who tells a birdwatching enthusiast, who tells a neighbour, who tells a journalist, who tells an editor, who includes a brief mention in a magazine article read by a member of the public. At each stage the speaker is sincere and does her best to relay the information accurately. No single link introduces an outright falsehood. Yet by the time the report reaches its final recipient, the cumulative effect of minor simplifications, paraphrases, and losses of nuance at each stage has transformed a precise scientific observation into a vague and substantially misleading claim. Each individual transmission satisfies the conditions, but the chain as a whole has degraded the information below the threshold of justification.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved short chains of two or three links, where cumulative degradation was negligible. The definition required that no link introduce distortion, but it conceived of distortion in binary terms — present or absent — and thereby failed to account for the gradual, sub-threshold losses of fidelity that compound over longer chains until the resulting testimony, though never overtly falsified, no longer warrants belief.
passage:
It is a familiar feature of our epistemic life that much of what we know reaches us not directly from an original source but through a chain of intermediaries. Consider several cases in which such chains operate successfully. A woman tells her husband that she saw a fox in the garden that morning; the husband mentions this to a colleague at work; the colleague relays it to his wife over dinner. At each link the speaker is sincere and competent, and the information arrives at its destination intact. A scientist publishes a finding in a peer-reviewed journal; a textbook author faithfully summarises it; a lecturer conveys the summary to her students without alteration. A diplomat relays a foreign leader's stated position to his own head of state, who in turn communicates it to a parliamentary committee, and the message passes through two intermediaries without material distortion. From these cases one might propose that a hearer at the end of a testimonial chain is justified in believing that p provided the original speaker was justified in asserting that p, each subsequent speaker sincerely and accurately relays what the preceding speaker asserted, and no link in the chain introduces distortion or falsehood.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose an ornithologist in the field observes a rare bird and records a meticulous description of its plumage, call, and preferred habitat. She communicates her observation to a colleague, who mentions it to a birdwatching enthusiast, who tells a neighbour, who tells a journalist, who tells an editor, who includes a brief notice in a popular magazine read by a member of the general public. At each stage the speaker is sincere and endeavours to relay the information faithfully. No single link introduces an outright falsehood or a gross distortion. Yet by the time the report reaches its final recipient — having passed through six intermediaries — the cumulative effect of minor simplifications, inadvertent paraphrases, and small losses of nuance at each stage has transformed a precise scientific observation into a vague and substantially misleading claim. The original description of a bird with olive-green upper-parts and a distinctive two-note call has become merely a report of an unusual green bird that made a strange noise. Every individual transmission satisfies the stated conditions, yet the chain as a whole has degraded the content below the threshold at which belief is warranted. The background cases, it now appears, involved short chains of two or three links, where the cumulative loss was negligible. The definition treated fidelity in binary terms — each link either distorts or does not — and thereby failed to reckon with the possibility that individually imperceptible losses of precision may compound across many links until the testimony, though never overtly falsified at any single point, no longer sustains justified belief.

background_cases: A friend sincerely tells one that the chemist on the high street closes at six o'clock, having checked the hours posted on the door that very morning; one is justified in accepting this.
A neighbour sincerely reports that she saw a delivery van outside one's house at noon; one has no reason to doubt her and is justified in believing her.
A fellow passenger on a train sincerely tells one that the next stop is Didcot Parkway, having travelled this route many times; one is justified in relying upon his word.
definition: A hearer H is justified in believing that p on the basis of S's testimony if and only if: (1) S asserts that p to H; (2) S sincerely believes that p; and (3) S intends to communicate the truth to H.
conditions: S asserts that p to H
S sincerely believes that p
S intends to communicate the truth to H
counterexample: A well-meaning but profoundly confused amateur astronomer sincerely tells his friend that the star Betelgeuse is approximately fifty light-years from Earth, genuinely believing this and intending nothing but to share what he takes to be the truth. In fact Betelgeuse is roughly seven hundred light-years distant, and the amateur's belief rests upon a thorough misunderstanding of the relevant data. He is perfectly sincere and wholly without deceptive intent, yet his testimony does not justify his friend in believing the distance to be fifty light-years. Sincerity, it appears, is necessary but far from sufficient for testimonial warrant; what is additionally required is that the testifier possess some adequate basis for the belief he communicates.
abductive_insight: Each of the background cases involved a speaker who was not merely sincere but who also possessed a competent basis for the belief — recent first-hand observation, habitual familiarity with a route, or direct perceptual evidence. The definition captured sincerity whilst silently depending upon competence, which happened to be present in every motivating case but was never made an explicit condition.
passage:
It is natural to suppose that sincerity is the cardinal virtue of testimony: if a speaker honestly tells us what she believes, intending to put us in possession of the truth, then — so the thought runs — we are justified in accepting her word. Several everyday cases appear to support this view. A friend sincerely tells one that the chemist on the high street closes at six o'clock, having checked the posted hours that very morning; one is justified in accepting this. A neighbour sincerely reports that she saw a delivery van outside one's house at midday; one has no reason to doubt her and accepts her word. A fellow passenger on a train sincerely tells one that the next stop is Didcot Parkway, having travelled the route many times; one is justified in relying upon him. From these cases one might extract a simple analysis: a hearer is justified in believing that p on the basis of a speaker's testimony provided the speaker asserts that p, sincerely believes it, and intends to communicate the truth.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Imagine a well-meaning but profoundly confused amateur astronomer who sincerely tells a friend that the star Betelgeuse is approximately fifty light-years from Earth. He genuinely believes this; he intends nothing other than to share what he takes to be a fascinating astronomical fact. In truth, Betelgeuse is roughly seven hundred light-years distant, and the amateur's belief rests upon a thorough misunderstanding of a half-remembered popular science article. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied — he asserts the proposition, he sincerely believes it, and he intends to communicate the truth — yet it seems clear that his friend is not justified in believing, on the strength of this testimony alone, that Betelgeuse is fifty light-years away. The testimony is perfectly sincere and wholly without deceptive intent, but it is also perfectly wrong, and its wrongness is not a matter of bad luck but of the testifier's lack of competence in the relevant domain. What the background cases had in common, and what the definition failed to make explicit, is that each speaker possessed not merely sincerity but also an adequate epistemic basis for the belief communicated — recent first-hand observation, habitual familiarity, direct perceptual evidence. The analysis captured sincerity whilst tacitly depending upon competence, which happened to be present in every motivating example but was never elevated to the status of an explicit necessary condition.

background_cases: A historian consults a letter written by a Victorian statesman, preserved in a university archive with a clear chain of custody, and relies upon it as evidence of the statesman's views.
A solicitor examines a signed and witnessed will, held by a firm of solicitors since its execution, and treats it as an authoritative record of the testator's intentions.
A genealogist consults a parish register of births and deaths, maintained continuously since the seventeenth century, and accepts its entries as reliable records of the relevant events.
definition: A reader R is justified in accepting the content of a written document D as testimony that p if and only if: (1) D purports to be authored by a specified person or institution; (2) D has been preserved in conditions that would normally ensure its integrity; (3) D bears the conventional marks of authenticity appropriate to its type (signatures, seals, official formatting); and (4) R has no specific reason to suspect that D has been tampered with.
conditions: D purports to be authored by a specified person or institution
D has been preserved in conditions that would normally ensure its integrity
D bears the conventional marks of authenticity appropriate to its type
R has no specific reason to suspect that D has been tampered with
counterexample: A skilful forger produces a letter purportedly written by a minor eighteenth-century poet, expressing views on a matter of literary controversy. The forgery is executed on period-appropriate paper, in a hand that closely imitates the poet's known script, and is planted in a provincial archive amongst genuine papers, where it is discovered by a literary scholar. The document purports to be authored by the poet, it has been preserved in archival conditions, it bears all the conventional marks of authenticity — the handwriting, the paper, the style of address — and the scholar has no specific reason to suspect tampering. Every condition of the analysis is satisfied, yet the document is a fabrication, and the scholar's acceptance of its contents is not justified in the way that acceptance of a genuine document would be.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved documents whose authenticity was secured not merely by outward marks and preservation conditions but by the genuine causal history connecting the document to its purported author. The definition attended only to surface indicators of authenticity — marks, preservation, and the absence of suspicion — without requiring that the document in fact originate from the source it claims, thereby rendering it powerless against a sufficiently competent forgery.
passage:
Written documents occupy a distinctive place in our testimonial practices. When we consult a letter, a legal instrument, or a parish register, we treat the document as a kind of preserved testimony — the words of a speaker set down in a durable medium and transmitted across time rather than across conversational space. Consider several cases which illustrate the conditions under which we ordinarily regard such reliance as justified. A historian consults a letter written by a Victorian statesman, preserved in a university archive with a clear and well-documented chain of custody, and relies upon it as evidence of the statesman's political views. A solicitor examines a will, duly signed and witnessed, held by a firm of solicitors since the date of its execution, and treats it as an authoritative record of the testator's final intentions. A genealogist consults a parish register of births and deaths, maintained continuously by the church since the seventeenth century, and accepts its entries as reliable testimony of the events they record. One might, from these examples, propose the following analysis: a reader is justified in accepting the content of a document as testimony that p provided the document purports to be authored by a specified person or institution, has been preserved in conditions ensuring its integrity, bears the conventional marks of authenticity appropriate to its type, and the reader has no specific reason to suspect tampering.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a skilful forger produces a letter purportedly written by a minor eighteenth-century poet, expressing views on a matter of current literary controversy. The forgery is executed on period-appropriate paper, in a hand that closely imitates the poet's known script, employing the idiom and conventions of the period with painstaking accuracy. The forger plants the document in a provincial archive amongst a collection of genuine papers, where it is in due course discovered by a literary scholar. The document purports to be authored by the poet; it has been preserved in archival conditions that would normally ensure integrity; it bears every conventional mark of authenticity — the handwriting, the paper, the style of salutation and subscription; and the scholar has no specific reason to suspect that it has been tampered with. All four conditions of our analysis are met, yet the document is a fabrication from first to last, and the scholar's acceptance of its contents is not epistemically justified in the manner that acceptance of a genuine document would be. What the background cases shared, and what the analysis failed to capture, is that the documents in question were not merely marked with the outward signs of authenticity but were in fact causally connected to their purported authors. The definition attended to surface indicators — marks, seals, preservation, the absence of suspicion — whilst neglecting the requirement that the document genuinely originate from the source it claims. Against a sufficiently competent forger, surface criteria are simply powerless.

background_cases: Three independent witnesses to a traffic accident each separately report that the car which caused the collision was red; the convergence of their testimony strengthens the case that the car was indeed red.

Two astronomers working at different observatories independently report observing the same transient celestial event; the agreement of their reports is taken as strong evidence that the event occurred.

A news story is corroborated by two journalists who conducted their investigations separately and arrived at the same conclusions; the editor regards the convergence as grounds for confidence in the story's accuracy.

Four patients of different physicians in different cities independently report the same unusual side-effect of a medication; the convergence is treated as significant evidence that the side-effect is genuine.
definition: A set of testimonies T₁, T₂, ... Tₙ that p provides strong justification for believing that p if and only if: (1) each Tᵢ independently asserts that p; (2) the testimonies agree in their content; and (3) the number of agreeing testimonies is sufficiently large (at least two).
conditions: Each testimony Tᵢ independently asserts that p
The testimonies agree in their content
The number of agreeing testimonies is sufficiently large
counterexample: In a community where a particular racial prejudice is deeply entrenched, five witnesses to an altercation between two men independently tell the police that the man belonging to the stigmatised group was the aggressor. Each witness gives her account separately; the accounts agree in content; and the number of concurring testimonies is well above two. Yet the agreement among the witnesses is explained not by their having independently observed the same event and arrived at the same judgement, but by their sharing the same prejudice, which predisposes each of them to interpret an ambiguous situation in accordance with a pre-existing stereotype. The conditions of the analysis are satisfied, but the convergence is epistemically worthless — or worse — because it is produced by a common bias rather than by independent sensitivity to the facts.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved witnesses or observers whose agreement was underwritten by independent causal contact with the same objective state of affairs. The definition required only that the testimonies be given independently and agree in content, but it failed to require that the agreement be explained by the facts rather than by a shared bias or common cause other than the truth of the proposition — a distinction upon which the entire epistemic value of corroboration depends.
passage:
Among the most powerful sources of testimonial justification, it is widely supposed, is the convergence of independent witnesses. When several persons, each testifying on her own account, agree upon a single proposition, the probability that they are all mistaken or all lying is thought to be very much less than the probability that any one of them alone is in error. Several cases illustrate this principle at work. Three independent witnesses to a traffic accident each separately report that the car responsible for the collision was red; their convergence materially strengthens the case that the car was indeed red. Two astronomers at different observatories independently report observing the same transient celestial event; the agreement of their observations is taken as compelling evidence that the event actually occurred. A news story is corroborated by two journalists who pursued their investigations separately and arrived at the same findings; the editor treats their convergence as adequate grounds for publication. Four patients of different physicians in geographically distant cities independently report the same unusual side-effect of a newly introduced medication; the convergence is regarded as significant evidence that the side-effect is genuine. From these cases one might extract the following analysis: a set of testimonies provides strong justification for believing that p if each testimony independently asserts that p, the testimonies agree in content, and the number of agreeing testimonies is sufficiently large.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that in a community where a particular racial prejudice is deeply entrenched, five witnesses to an altercation between two men independently tell the police that the man belonging to the stigmatised group was the aggressor. Each witness gives her account separately; none has consulted the others; the five accounts agree in their essential content; and the number of concurring testimonies exceeds the minimum by a comfortable margin. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied. Yet the agreement among the witnesses is explained not by their having independently perceived the same objective facts and converged upon the same honest description, but by their sharing the same deeply held prejudice, which predisposes each of them to interpret an ambiguous confrontation in accordance with a pre-existing racial stereotype. The convergence is not merely unhelpful — it is positively misleading, for it mimics the outward form of genuine corroboration whilst being produced by a mechanism that has nothing to do with sensitivity to the truth. What the background cases had in common, and what the definition neglected to require, is that the witnesses' agreement was causally explained by their independent contact with the same objective state of affairs. The analysis demanded only independence and agreement, but the entire epistemic value of corroboration depends upon the further condition that the agreement be explained by the facts themselves rather than by some common cause — a shared prejudice, a cultural assumption, an institutional pressure — that operates independently of the truth of the proposition attested.

background_cases: One recalls having breakfasted this morning and can recount the items on one's plate; this seems a clear case of memory knowledge.
A scholar recalls the argument of a paper read last week, and her recollection accords with the text; we should wish to say she remembers it.
A traveller recounts the route he took through an unfamiliar city, and his account is confirmed by the map; again, we recognise genuine memorial knowledge.
definition: A subject S has memory knowledge that p if and only if: (1) S previously came to know that p through experience or inference; (2) S now has a conscious recollective impression that p, phenomenologically continuous with ordinary recall; (3) S's present belief that p is true; and (4) S's present belief is sustained by the retention of the earlier cognitive episode.
conditions: S previously came to know that p through experience or inference
S now has a conscious recollective impression that p, phenomenologically continuous with ordinary recall
S's present belief that p is true
S's present belief is sustained by the retention of the earlier cognitive episode
counterexample: Consider a subject who, owing to a neurological peculiarity, spontaneously generates a vivid and detailed recollective impression of having witnessed a motor accident at a particular crossroads last Tuesday. The impression possesses all the phenomenological marks of genuine recall — temporal location, sensory specificity, a sense of pastness — and, by sheer coincidence, a motor accident did occur at precisely that place and time, though the subject was in fact elsewhere. The subject's confabulation satisfies the definition: there is a recollective impression, the believed proposition is true, and the subject sincerely takes herself to be retaining an earlier experience. Yet we should not wish to call this memory knowledge.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involved not merely phenomenological continuity but a genuine historical connection between the original experience and the present recollection; the definition, in abstracting from particular cases to general conditions, failed to require that the recollective impression be produced by the very experience it purports to represent.
passage:
It is natural to suppose that we may arrive at a satisfactory analysis of memory knowledge by attending to its most familiar instances. When one recalls having breakfasted this morning and can recount the items on one's plate, or when a scholar recollects the argument of a paper read last week and her recollection accords with the text, or when a traveller recounts the route he took through an unfamiliar city and his account is confirmed by the map, we seem to have paradigmatic cases from which to extract the essential features of memorial knowledge. From such cases one might be led to propose that a subject S has memory knowledge that p provided that S previously came to know that p, that S now has a conscious recollective impression that p bearing the phenomenological character of ordinary recall, that S's present belief that p is true, and that S's belief is sustained by retention of the earlier cognitive episode.

Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject who, owing to a neurological peculiarity, spontaneously generates a vivid and detailed recollective impression of having witnessed a motor accident at a certain crossroads last Tuesday. The impression possesses every phenomenological mark of genuine recall — temporal location, sensory specificity, a felt sense of pastness — and, by a remarkable coincidence, a motor accident did occur at precisely that place and time, though the subject was in fact elsewhere and had no knowledge of it. She sincerely takes herself to be retaining an earlier experience; the believed proposition is true; and the impression is phenomenologically indistinguishable from ordinary recall. The definition appears to be satisfied, yet we should hardly wish to ascribe memory knowledge to this subject, for the recollective impression bears no genuine historical relation to the event it purports to represent. What the background cases shared, and what the definition failed to capture, was that in each instance the present recollection was produced by the very experience of which it is a recollection. In abstracting to general conditions, one lost the requirement of proper causal and historical provenance, without which the mere co-occurrence of a true belief and a recollective phenomenology does not amount to remembering.

background_cases: One learns a mathematical theorem in one's youth and, twenty years later, still assents to it; the content appears preserved intact.
A witness recalls the colour of a motor car involved in an accident, and her recall matches her original perception; the information has been retained.
A musician memorises a passage of score and, months later, can reproduce it note for note; the original knowledge has been carried forward without alteration.
definition: Memory preserves knowledge if and only if: (1) S knew that p at an earlier time t1; (2) S believes that p at a later time t2; (3) the content of S's belief at t2 is identical to the content known at t1; and (4) S's belief at t2 is causally sustained by the cognitive state that constituted knowledge at t1.
conditions: S knew that p at an earlier time t1
S believes that p at a later time t2
The content of S's belief at t2 is identical to the content known at t1
S's belief at t2 is causally sustained by the cognitive state that constituted knowledge at t1
counterexample: Consider a child who learns that her grandfather was a kindly man, on the testimony of her parents. Over many years the memory subtly transforms: the originally reported kindliness amalgamates with her own imagined scenes, and her recollection now represents the grandfather as having performed specific acts of generosity that were never reported and never occurred. The content has been altered by the very processes of retention, yet she takes herself to be preserving the same knowledge she originally acquired. The causal chain is unbroken, but the content at t2 is no longer identical with the content at t1.
abductive_insight: The background cases were each ones in which the content happened to be of a kind resistant to transformation — mathematical, perceptual, or formally structured — and the analysis mistakenly generalised this stability into a necessary condition, overlooking that memory routinely and imperceptibly reshapes its own contents.
passage:
We are inclined to regard memory as a preservative faculty — one which retains what was earlier known and delivers it, at a later time, in substantially the same form. The inclination is encouraged by familiar cases. One learns a mathematical theorem in one's youth and, twenty years on, still assents to it; the content seems preserved without alteration. A witness recalls the colour of a motor car, and her recall matches her original perception. A musician memorises a passage of score and can reproduce it months later, note for note. From such cases one might extract the following analysis: memory preserves knowledge provided that S knew that p at an earlier time, that S believes that p at a later time, that the content of S's later belief is identical to the content originally known, and that S's later belief is causally sustained by the earlier cognitive state.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a child who learns, on the testimony of her parents, that her grandfather was a kindly man. Over many years the memory subtly transforms: the originally reported kindliness amalgamates with imagined scenes of her own devising, and her recollection now represents the grandfather as having performed specific acts of generosity that were never in fact reported and never occurred. The causal chain from the original testimony to the present belief is unbroken, yet the content has been altered by the very processes of retention. She sincerely takes herself to be preserving the same knowledge she originally acquired, but what she now believes is not what she originally knew. The background cases, it emerges, were each ones in which the content was of a kind peculiarly resistant to transformation — mathematical, narrowly perceptual, or formally structured. The analysis mistakenly elevated this incidental stability into a necessary condition, and in so doing obscured the characteristic tendency of memory to reshape, supplement, and sometimes silently replace its own contents whilst maintaining the subjective appearance of faithful preservation.

background_cases: One vividly recalls posting a letter yesterday, is fully confident in this recollection, and did in fact post the letter; we should say one remembers doing so.
A friend recounts, with great vividness and assurance, the details of a conversation held last month, and the details are confirmed by a recording; this is genuine remembering.

A student recalls the substance of a lecture delivered a fortnight ago; her recollection is vivid, she is confident it is accurate, and it accords with the lecturer's notes; she remembers the lecture.
definition: S remembers that p (as opposed to merely seeming to remember) if and only if: (1) S has a vivid recollective experience as of p; (2) S is confident that this experience represents a genuine past episode; (3) p is true; and (4) S's recollective experience is not the product of deliberate imagination.
conditions: S has a vivid recollective experience as of p
S is confident that this experience represents a genuine past episode
p is true
S's recollective experience is not the product of deliberate imagination
counterexample: Suppose that a subject has undergone a therapeutic procedure in which a detailed and vivid false memory of having visited a particular seaside town in childhood has been implanted by suggestion. The subject now has a recollective experience of extraordinary vividness concerning this visit; she is entirely confident that it represents a genuine episode from her past; the proposition that she visited the town is, by coincidence, true — her parents took her there as an infant, though she has no organic memory of it; and the experience was not produced by her own deliberate imagination but by the therapist's intervention. All four conditions are met, yet this is not a case of remembering.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involved a recollective experience generated by the subject's own cognitive retention of the original event, but the analysis omitted any requirement concerning the aetiology of the recollective experience, leaving it open for externally implanted impressions to satisfy the stated conditions.
passage:
The distinction between genuinely remembering and merely seeming to remember is of considerable epistemological importance, and one naturally seeks to draw it by reference to the features most conspicuous in clear cases. When one vividly recalls posting a letter yesterday and is confident in this recollection, and did in fact post the letter, we should say without hesitation that one remembers doing so. When a friend recounts with great vividness and assurance the details of a conversation held last month, and these details are confirmed by a recording, this too is genuine remembering. When a student recalls the substance of a lecture, her recollection is vivid, she is confident, and it accords with the lecturer's notes — she remembers the lecture. From these cases one might propose that S remembers that p provided that S has a vivid recollective experience as of p, is confident that this experience represents a genuine past episode, p is true, and the experience is not the product of deliberate imagination.

Yet a case may be described which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject has undergone a therapeutic procedure in which a detailed and vivid false memory of having visited a particular seaside town in childhood has been implanted by suggestion. She now possesses a recollective experience of extraordinary vividness; she is entirely confident it represents a genuine episode from her past; and the proposition that she visited the town is, by a notable coincidence, true — her parents took her there as an infant, though she retains no organic memory of the visit. The experience was not produced by her own deliberate imagination but by the therapist's intervention. Every condition of the proposed analysis is satisfied, yet we should not wish to say she remembers visiting the town. What the background cases shared, and what the definition neglected to require, was that the recollective experience be generated by the subject's own cognitive retention of the original event. By omitting any condition on the proper aetiology of the experience, the analysis left room for externally induced impressions to masquerade as genuine memory.

background_cases: A clerk recalls the faces of customers served during the day, and each recollection proves accurate upon review of the shop's photographic record; the consistency of her recall attests to its reliability.
A historian recounts from memory the dates of several treaties, and each date is confirmed by documentary evidence; the consistent correctness of these recollections gives us reason to trust them.
One recalls the sequence of errands one ran last Saturday, and each item is corroborated by receipts and diary entries; the coherent and consistent recall is taken as reliable.
definition: S's memory that p is reliable if and only if: (1) S consistently recalls that p across multiple occasions; (2) S's recalled content p is true; (3) S's recall is not the product of external prompting or suggestion; and (4) S has no reason to doubt the accuracy of the recollection.
conditions: S consistently recalls that p across multiple occasions
S's recalled content p is true
S's recall is not the product of external prompting or suggestion
S has no reason to doubt the accuracy of the recollection
counterexample: Suppose a man witnessed an altercation in the street but, owing to poor lighting and an obstructed vantage point, misperceived the aggressor's coat as red when it was in fact brown. He has consistently recalled the coat as red on every occasion of recollection, with no external prompting and no reason for doubt. His recall is consistent, he is free from suggestion, and he has no grounds for suspicion — yet the recalled content is false, and its consistency is owed not to reliability but to the faithful preservation of an original error. Now alter the case slightly: suppose the aggressor happened to be wearing a red coat beneath the brown one, visible only from another angle, so that the proposition 'the aggressor wore a red coat' is, in a sense, true. All four conditions are then met, yet we should hardly call this reliable memory, for the truth of the recollection is entirely accidental.
abductive_insight: The background cases were ones in which the original perception was itself accurate, so that consistency of recall tracked truth in virtue of preserving a veridical starting point; the analysis failed to require that the original cognitive episode from which memory derives was itself reliable.
passage:
It is tempting to identify the reliability of memory with the consistency of recall, for in the most familiar cases the two go together. A clerk recalls the faces of the day's customers, and each recollection is confirmed by the shop's photographic record; the consistency of her recall attests to its dependability. A historian recounts from memory the dates of several treaties, and each is verified by documentary evidence. One recalls the sequence of errands run last Saturday, and every item is corroborated by receipts and diary entries. From such cases one might conclude that S's memory that p is reliable provided that S consistently recalls that p, that p is true, that the recall is not the product of external suggestion, and that S has no reason to doubt the recollection.

A case may, however, be described which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man witnessed an altercation in the street but, owing to poor lighting and an obstructed vantage point, misperceived the aggressor's coat as red when it was in fact brown. He has consistently recalled the coat as red on every occasion, without prompting and without doubt. His recall is admirably consistent — yet it consistently preserves an original error. Now suppose, by an improbable but not impossible turn, that the aggressor was in fact wearing a red garment beneath the brown, visible only from another angle, so that the proposition recalled is, in a manner of speaking, true. All four conditions of the analysis are then satisfied, yet we should hardly call this reliable memory, for the truth of the recollection is entirely accidental. What the background cases shared was that the original perceptions from which the memories derived were themselves accurate, so that the consistency of recall tracked truth precisely because it preserved veridical starting points. The analysis, in omitting any requirement on the reliability or accuracy of the original cognitive episode, mistook a symptom of reliability — consistency — for its ground.

background_cases: One recalls being startled by a clap of thunder last evening; the present recollection is causally connected to the original experience, and we recognise this as genuine memory.
A soldier recalls the sound of gunfire during a battle years ago; the recollection is causally traceable, through normal mnemonic processes, to the original auditory experience.
A child recalls the taste of a particular sweet given to her by an aunt; the recollection is sustained by the same neural trace laid down during the original tasting.
definition: S genuinely remembers an event E if and only if: (1) S experienced E at an earlier time; (2) S now has a recollective representation of E; (3) S's recollective representation is causally connected to the original experience of E; and (4) S's recollective representation is broadly accurate with respect to E.
conditions: S experienced E at an earlier time
S now has a recollective representation of E
S's recollective representation is causally connected to the original experience of E
S's recollective representation is broadly accurate with respect to E
counterexample: Suppose a woman experienced a particular sunset whilst travelling in Italy. She subsequently forgot the event entirely — the original memory trace decayed beyond retrieval. Years later, she comes upon a detailed diary entry she wrote at the time, reads it, and thereby forms a new, vivid recollective representation of the sunset, complete with sensory imagery. Her present representation is causally connected to the original experience, for it was the experience that caused her to write the diary, and the diary caused her present recollection. The representation is broadly accurate. She experienced the event. All four conditions are satisfied. Yet there is a strong intuition that this is not genuine remembering but rather re-learning — the causal chain passed entirely outside the mnemonic system and re-entered through testimony.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involved a causal chain that remained internal to the subject's mnemonic faculties from the time of experience to the time of recall; the analysis required only causal connection, without specifying that the connection must be sustained through the appropriate mnemonic channels.
passage:
It is widely supposed that what distinguishes genuine memory from mere true belief about the past is the existence of an appropriate causal connection between the original experience and the present recollection. One recalls being startled by a clap of thunder last evening, and the recollection is causally continuous with the original experience; a soldier recalls the sound of gunfire during a battle, and the recollection is traceable through normal mnemonic processes to the original auditory experience; a child recalls the taste of a particular sweet, and the recollection is sustained by the neural trace laid down during the tasting. From these cases one might extract the following analysis: S genuinely remembers an event E provided that S experienced E, that S now has a recollective representation of E, that this representation is causally connected to the original experience, and that it is broadly accurate.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a woman experienced a particular sunset whilst travelling in Italy but subsequently forgot the event entirely — the original trace decayed beyond retrieval. Years later she discovers a detailed diary entry she wrote at the time, reads it, and thereby forms a vivid recollective representation of the sunset, replete with sensory imagery. Her present representation is causally connected to the original experience, for it was the experience that moved her to write the diary, and the diary in turn caused her present recollection. The representation is broadly accurate, and she did experience the event. All four conditions appear to be met. Yet we should hesitate to call this genuine remembering; it seems rather a case of re-learning or reconstruction, for the causal chain passed entirely outside the mnemonic system and re-entered through what amounts to testimony from her earlier self. What the background cases shared was that the causal connection remained internal to the subject's own mnemonic faculties throughout — from the laying down of the trace to its retrieval. The analysis, in requiring only that some causal connection obtain, failed to specify that the connection must be of the right kind, sustained through the appropriate mnemonic channels rather than through external intermediaries.

background_cases: A pianist's fingers find the correct keys without conscious deliberation; this is habit memory, a bodily competence retained from practice, containing no propositional content about the music.
One ties one's shoelaces each morning without attending to the procedure; this habitual skill is retained from childhood learning and involves no recollection of particular episodes.
A cyclist maintains balance without thinking about the principles of equilibrium; the skill is a retained capacity, quite unlike recollective memory of past events.
definition: Habit memory is to be distinguished from recollective memory as follows: (1) habit memory is a retained capacity to perform an action; (2) it is exercised without conscious recollection of any particular past episode; (3) it involves no propositional content — no belief that such-and-such is the case; and (4) it is acquired through repeated practice rather than through a single episode of learning.
conditions: Habit memory is a retained capacity to perform an action
It is exercised without conscious recollection of any particular past episode
It involves no propositional content
It is acquired through repeated practice rather than through a single episode of learning
counterexample: Consider a physician who, through years of diagnostic practice, has acquired the capacity to examine a patient and arrive at a correct diagnosis with great rapidity and without consciously recollecting any particular past case. She does not deliberate; the diagnosis seems to arise from a practised facility rather than from explicit reasoning. Yet her skill is not merely motor — it essentially involves propositional content, for what she arrives at is a judgement that such-and-such is the case. She cannot exercise the skill without thereby forming beliefs. The capacity was acquired through repeated practice, it is exercised without conscious recollection of particular episodes, and it is a retained ability to perform an action — yet it is saturated with propositional content, and we should be uncertain whether to classify it as habit memory or as something more akin to recollective knowledge.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all ones in which the retained skill was purely motor or bodily, so that the absence of propositional content followed trivially from the nature of the activity; the analysis mistakenly treated this absence as a defining mark of habit memory rather than as an incidental feature of the examples chosen.
passage:
Philosophers have long recognised a distinction between habit memory and recollective memory. The pianist whose fingers find the correct keys without conscious deliberation, the person who ties shoelaces each morning without attending to the procedure, the cyclist who maintains balance without thinking about the principles of equilibrium — these are paradigmatic cases of habit memory, and they contrast sharply with one's recollective memory of, say, a conversation held last week. From such cases one might extract a definition: habit memory is a retained capacity to perform an action, exercised without conscious recollection of any particular past episode, involving no propositional content, and acquired through repeated practice rather than through a single episode of learning.

Yet a case may be constructed which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a physician who, through years of diagnostic practice, has acquired the capacity to examine a patient and arrive at a correct diagnosis with great rapidity and without consciously recollecting any particular past case. She does not deliberate; the diagnosis emerges from a practised facility rather than from explicit reasoning. The capacity was acquired through repeated practice; it is exercised without conscious recollection of particular episodes; and it is indisputably a retained ability to perform an action. In all these respects it resembles the pianist's skill. Yet her skill is not merely motor — it essentially involves propositional content, for what she arrives at is a judgement that such-and-such is the case, and she cannot exercise the capacity without thereby forming beliefs about the patient's condition. We find ourselves uncertain whether to classify this as habit memory or as something more akin to recollective knowledge retained in a dispositional form. The background cases, it emerges, were all ones in which the skill was purely motor or bodily, so that the absence of propositional content followed trivially from the nature of the activity. The analysis elevated this incidental feature into a defining characteristic, thereby obscuring the existence of practised capacities that are at once habitual in their exercise and thoroughly propositional in their content.

background_cases: One recalls attending one's own graduation ceremony — the memory is episodic, grounded in personal experience, and carries a distinctive first-person phenomenology.
A woman remembers the birth of her first child; the memory is saturated with experiential detail and is unmistakably her own.
One recalls the layout of one's childhood home; the knowledge is bound up with personal experience and could not have been acquired by testimony alone.
definition: Episodic (or personal) memory is to be distinguished from merely semantic memory as follows: (1) the content of the memory derives from S's own past experience; (2) S has a phenomenological sense of re-experiencing or reliving the original episode; (3) the memory carries a first-person perspective — it is experienced as one's own; and (4) the memory includes contextual details (time, place, affect) not present in bare semantic knowledge.
conditions: The content of the memory derives from S's own past experience
S has a phenomenological sense of re-experiencing or reliving the original episode
The memory carries a first-person perspective
The memory includes contextual details not present in bare semantic knowledge
counterexample: Suppose a man reads a vivid first-person account of the liberation of a particular city during the war. Over many years the narrative becomes deeply embedded in his cognitive life, and he comes to have what seems to him a genuine episodic memory of being present at the liberation — complete with sensory imagery, temporal and spatial context, emotional affect, and a first-person perspective. He was never present at the event; the content derives entirely from reading. Yet because the original reading was itself an experience, and the memory does in a sense derive from his own past experience — the experience of reading — the first condition is met in a deflationary way. The phenomenology of re-experiencing is present; the first-person perspective is present; and the contextual details are richly supplied. All four conditions are satisfied, yet what the man possesses is not genuine episodic memory of the liberation but semantic knowledge that has acquired a false experiential packaging.
abductive_insight: The background cases were ones in which the experiential origin and the experiential content of the memory were of the same event, but the analysis failed to require that the experience from which the memory derives be an experience of the very event that the memory purports to represent.
passage:
The distinction between episodic and semantic memory is of considerable philosophical interest, for it bears upon the nature of personal knowledge and the first-person perspective. When one recalls attending one's own graduation ceremony, the memory is grounded in personal experience and carries a distinctive first-person phenomenology that mere propositional knowledge about the event could not possess. When a woman remembers the birth of her first child, the memory is saturated with experiential detail and is unmistakably her own. When one recalls the layout of one's childhood home, the knowledge is inseparable from personal experience. From such cases one might propose that episodic memory is distinguished from semantic memory by the following conditions: the content derives from S's own past experience; S has a phenomenological sense of re-experiencing the original episode; the memory carries a first-person perspective; and it includes contextual details not present in bare semantic knowledge.

There is, however, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man reads a vivid first-person account of the liberation of a certain city during the war. Over many years the narrative becomes deeply embedded, and he comes to possess what seems to him a genuine episodic memory of having been present at the liberation — complete with sensory imagery, temporal and spatial context, emotional colouring, and a first-person perspective. He was never present; the content derives entirely from his reading. Yet because the original reading was itself an experience he underwent, the condition that the content derive from his own past experience is met, albeit in a deflationary sense. The phenomenological, perspectival, and contextual conditions are all satisfied as well. What the man possesses, however, is not genuine episodic memory of the liberation but semantic knowledge that has acquired a spurious experiential dress. The background cases were ones in which the experiential origin and the experiential content of the memory coincided — the experience from which the memory derived was an experience of the very event remembered. The analysis failed to require this coincidence, and thereby left room for semantically acquired knowledge to masquerade, through the accretion of false experiential trappings, as personal episodic memory.

background_cases: One forms a well-justified belief that the chemist's shop closes at six o'clock, on the basis of having checked the posted hours; years later one still believes it, and we are inclined to say the belief remains justified through memory.
A student learns, on the basis of careful study, that the Battle of Hastings occurred in 1066; decades later she retains the belief, and we credit memory with preserving her justification.

A scientist forms a justified belief in a particular experimental result on the basis of her own careful observations; years later she recalls the result, and we are disposed to say her belief remains justified.
definition: A belief retained through memory preserves its justification if and only if: (1) S was justified in believing that p at the time of original acquisition; (2) S has retained the belief that p continuously since the original acquisition; (3) S has not subsequently acquired defeating evidence against p; and (4) S has no present reason to doubt the belief.
conditions: S was justified in believing that p at the time of original acquisition
S has retained the belief that p continuously since the original acquisition
S has not subsequently acquired defeating evidence against p
S has no present reason to doubt the belief
counterexample: Suppose a man forms the belief that a certain bridge is structurally sound, on the basis of what he takes to be a thorough engineering report. In fact, the report was fraudulent and contained fabricated data; the man's original justification was, unbeknownst to him, fatally flawed. He retains the belief for many years, acquires no defeating evidence, and has no reason for doubt. He has, moreover, forgotten the specific grounds on which he originally formed the belief — he knows only that he once had good reason to accept it. All four conditions of the analysis are satisfied: he was (so far as he could tell) justified at the time of acquisition, he has retained the belief continuously, he has encountered no defeaters, and he has no present grounds for doubt. Yet if the original justification was in fact no justification at all, it is difficult to see how memory could have preserved what was never genuinely present.
abductive_insight: The background cases were ones in which the original justification was not merely apparently adequate but genuinely so; the analysis required only that S was justified at the time of acquisition, without distinguishing between justification that was genuine and justification that merely seemed adequate to the subject, and thus allowed memory to appear to preserve a justification that never truly existed.
passage:
It is commonly held that memory serves not only to preserve beliefs but also to preserve their justification. When one forms a well-justified belief that the chemist's shop closes at six, on the basis of having checked the posted hours, and retains this belief for years, we are inclined to say the justification carries forward through memory. A student who learned on the basis of careful study that the Battle of Hastings occurred in 1066 retains, decades later, a justified belief. A scientist who formed a justified belief in an experimental result on the strength of her own careful observations may recall the result years hence with justification intact. From these cases one might propose that a belief retained through memory preserves its justification provided that S was justified at the time of original acquisition, that S has continuously retained the belief, that S has acquired no defeating evidence, and that S has no present reason for doubt.

A case may, however, be described which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man forms the belief that a certain bridge is structurally sound, on the basis of what he takes to be a thorough engineering report. The report is in fact fraudulent, containing fabricated data, so that his original justification is, unbeknownst to him, fatally defective. He retains the belief for many years, encounters no contrary evidence, and has no reason for doubt. He has, moreover, forgotten the particular grounds on which he originally formed the belief; he knows only that he once had what seemed good reason. Every condition of the analysis appears to be met, yet it is exceedingly difficult to see how memory could have preserved a justification that was never genuinely present. The background cases were each ones in which the original justification was not merely apparently but genuinely adequate — the posted hours were accurate, the historical record was sound, the observations were careful and veridical. The analysis, in requiring only that S was justified at the time of acquisition without attending to whether that justification was merely apparent or real, allowed for the possibility that memory might seem to preserve what in truth never existed, thereby conflating the subjective appearance of justified retention with the genuine article.

background_cases: We know that 2 + 3 = 5 without needing to count physical objects; the knowledge arises from grasping the arithmetical concepts.
We know that if A is taller than B and B is taller than C, then A is taller than C, without needing to measure anyone.
We know that all bachelors are unmarried without conducting a survey; the meaning of the terms suffices.
definition: A proposition is known a priori if and only if (i) the subject grasps the relevant concepts, (ii) the grasp of those concepts is sufficient for the subject to recognise the truth of the proposition, and (iii) no empirical investigation is needed to confirm the proposition.
conditions: subject grasps the relevant concepts
concept-grasp sufficient to recognise truth
no empirical investigation needed
counterexample: Consider the proposition that there is no largest prime number. A subject may fully grasp the concepts of 'prime number' and 'largest,' yet the recognition that there is no largest prime requires not merely concept-grasp but a non-trivial proof — Euclid's argument involving the construction of a new prime. Concept-grasp alone is insufficient; a further act of reasoning is needed. All three conditions appear met on a superficial reading, yet the second condition is satisfied only in a misleading sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve propositions whose truth is immediately transparent upon grasping the concepts. The definition failed to distinguish immediate conceptual recognition from recognition requiring substantive proof, thereby admitting cases where the route from concept-grasp to truth is non-trivial.
passage:
Consider the following cases of a priori knowledge. We know that two and three make five without needing to count physical objects; the knowledge arises from our grasp of the arithmetical concepts. We know that if A is taller than B and B is taller than C, then A is taller than C, without measuring any persons; the relation of transitivity is transparent upon reflection. We know that all bachelors are unmarried without conducting a census; the meanings of the terms suffice. From such cases one might extract the following analysis: a proposition is known a priori if and only if the subject grasps the relevant concepts, the grasp of those concepts is sufficient for recognising the truth of the proposition, and no empirical investigation is needed.

Yet this analysis, for all its plausibility, may be challenged. Consider the proposition that there is no largest prime number. A subject may fully grasp the concepts of 'prime number' and 'largest,' yet the truth of the proposition is far from immediately apparent. Its recognition requires a non-trivial proof — Euclid's celebrated argument involving the construction of a number which must have a prime factor exceeding any given prime. Here, concept-grasp alone is insufficient; a further act of sustained reasoning is needed. The background cases from which we drew our definition all involved truths whose recognition was immediate upon grasping the relevant concepts. The definition failed to distinguish between immediate conceptual recognition and the recognition of truths which, though a priori, require elaborate proof. By conflating the two, the analysis either overstates the power of concept-grasp or admits, under the same heading, cases of very different epistemic character.

background_cases: The truth that nothing can be simultaneously red and green all over is known without empirical verification.

known without appeal to experience

The definition did not account for the possibility that the a priori status of a proposition might be relative to a conceptual framework and revisable upon conceptual change.

background_cases: A child learns that 7 + 5 = 12 and can verify it by counting, but also comes to see its truth through understanding arithmetic.

background_cases: The proposition 'Either it is raining or it is not raining' is a logical truth known a priori; its truth depends on the logical connectives alone.

The truth depends not only on logical form but on whether the non-logical vocabulary picks out a coherent concept.
